title: 2024 OIAI Audit Report on Ukraine Regional Refugee Response
fieldwork: March to July 2023 (on-site in Poland, the Czech Republic and the Republic of Moldova 12 to 23 June)

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management and control processes were Partially Satisfactory, Improvement Needed, meaning that the weaknesses or deficiencies identified were unlikely to have a materially negative impact on the audited entity, area, activity or process.

observations.title: Strategy and legal framework for responding in non-programme countries
observations.summary_quote: There was no properly approved legal framework or formal strategy for UNICEF cooperation in programmes related to the response to the Ukrainian refugee and migrant crises in some countries, creating elevated risks to the effectiveness of the response.

observations.title: Monitoring and oversight of response activities
observations.summary_quote: The governance and oversight systems required to ensure the effectiveness and efficiency of UNICEF’s response to Ukraine refugee and migrant crises were not established.

observations.title: Planning for the response and transition strategy
observations.summary_quote: There was no transition strategy in place even though the response offices were set up in non-programme countries for an initial period of two years. In addition, there was a lack of oversight at a sub-national level of the commitments being made in the signed work plans, resulting in shortfalls between the commitments made and the funds available.

observations.title: Staff management
observations.summary_quote: There were significant increases in staffing of response offices without a formal staffing capacity and affordability analysis. There were also inadequate staff welfare mechanisms in response offices.

observations.title: Cash transfers to partners
observations.summary_quote: The response entailed disbursement of large sums of direct cash transfers to partners rated high risk because their financial capacity had not yet been assessed.

observations.title: Accounting for supplies
observations.summary_quote: Given the scale and complexity of the deployment for the response, ECARO might have lacked sufficient capacity and experience on specific aspects of financial accounting, leading to errors and delays in accounting for supplies.

observations.title: Prevention of sexual exploitation and abuse
observations.summary_quote: The results from monitoring related to PSEA were not systematically documented and communicated for action and follow-up. Further, partners’ PSEA assessments for subcontracted CSOs were not enforced.

observations.title: Accountability to affected populations
observations.summary_quote: Insufficient resourcing of AAP, late operationalization and insufficient capacity from the onset were some of the reasons why UNICEF was not yet fulfilling its commitment to hear the voices of affected populations.

observations.title: Programme monitoring
observations.summary_quote: the audit found that the preparation and implementation of a field monitoring plan was uneven and inconsistent across the responding offices, and thus the approach to field monitoring was not risk-based

observations.title: Results recording and reporting
observations.summary_quote: As SitReps become public documents, increased quality assurance mechanisms are required to minimize the reputational risk of inaccurate reporting on targets, results and achievements.

observations.title: Fraud risk management
observations.summary_quote: A fraud and misconduct risk assessment would provide a necessary foundation for determining risk tolerance levels and would help management identify the risks from fraud and misconduct, identify gaps or weaknesses in controls and implement commensurate mitigants and other appropriate preventive tools.

observations.title: Lessons learned and knowledge management
observations.summary_quote: There was insufficient evidence that lessons learned from previous UNICEF responses were utilized, and there was an absence of a systematic process of documenting learning from UNICEF’s response to the ongoing Ukraine refugee and migrant crises.

observations.title: Technology in emergencies
observations.summary_quote: OIAI was informed that there were numerous problems with the information technology systems that had not been previously used at the scale, that they were for the Ukraine crisis.

agreed_actions.quote: The Office of the Executive Director agrees to put in place and ensure the implementation of specific steps and guidance to ensure consistent and full compliance with Regulation 8.13 of UNICEF’s Financial Regulations and Rules

ECARO agrees to put in place appropriate mechanisms, including key performance indicators, to enable effective coordination, monitoring and oversight of the response offices’ financial and programmatic performance.
Take appropriate steps to ensure the regional response plan, Humanitarian Action for Children, government work plans and its own response plans are aligned.

agrees to undertake human resources capacity and affordability analyses and, based on the results of such analyses, implement a plan to realign staffing levels with the needs of the emergency response.
ECARO agrees to strengthen oversight on management of implementing partners’ fiduciary risks by ensuring that response and country offices follow a risk-based assurance plan
ECARO agrees to ensure that the appropriate mechanisms to vet, receive and record contributions in-kind are in place

ECARO agrees to establish mechanisms to ensure compliance with PSEA risk assessments and, in consultation with UNICEF headquarters, provide guidance on the assessment and action plans for downstream PSEA risks
establish adequate AAP governance and management systems that are appropriate to the context.
Support the development and implementation of risk-informed monitoring plans and monitor their implementation as part of the enhanced oversight and quality assurance mechanisms

ECARO agrees to finalize its online integrated and centralized reporting system and to enhance the collection, cleaning, reviewing and reporting of humanitarian performance data from all the 19 responding offices.
Put in place a regional risk management structure that will conduct regional fraud risk assessments at appropriate intervals, including the assessment of all significant or new programmes.
ECARO agrees to develop and implement knowledge management processes that guide the effective capture, organization, analysis and sharing of the knowledge acquired from crises in the region.

Programme Group, in coordination with the ICTD business partner, agrees to lead an exercise with relevant UNICEF offices to identify lessons learned from the use of HOPE and use these lessons to establish clear criteria on when HOPE should be utilized

noteworthy_practices.quote: The UNICEF Europe and Central Asia Regional Office (ECARO) proactively commissioned a number of assessments, including an independent evaluation of UNICEF’s response to the outflow of Ukrainian refugees, an operational review of humanitarian cash transfers (HCT) and social protection responses, and a regional compliance review on partnership management.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

title: 2024 OIAI Audit Report on the Kenya Country Office

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management or control processes were Partially Satisfactory, Improvement Needed, meaning that assessed governance, risk management or control processes were generally adequate and functioning but needed improvement

observations.title: Role of the Quality Assurance section
observations.summary_quote: The centralization of transaction processing activities in the Country Office’s Quality Assurance section has led to inefficiencies and bottlenecks that impacted operational and programmatic performance and blurred the lines between operational execution and oversight, potentially compromising effective risk management and internal control.

observations.title: Fraud risk management
observations.summary_quote: While the Country Office had established measures to identify and mitigate key fraud risks, there were opportunities to further strengthen its approach to preventing, detecting and responding to fraud.

observations.title: Cash transfers to implementing partners
observations.summary_quote: The audit team noted frequent low-value direct cash transfers, inefficient cash handling by some partners, inconsistencies between partners’ cash requests and financial reporting, and spending on expired grants. These issues stem primarily from weaknesses in partners’ financial management capacity and internal processes.

observations.title: HACT assurance activities
observations.summary_quote: There were delays in finalizing cash transfer assurance and monitoring activities and deficiencies in creating and following up action points. These challenges may compromise financial integrity and accountability in programme implementation.

observations.title: Service contracts
observations.summary_quote: Failure to involve the Human Resources (HR) section in development of terms of reference for a contract to provide HR services created legal, operational and SEA risks related to supplementary personnel hired through an external firm to perform essential programme activities.

observations.title: Civil Society Organization (CSO) partnerships
observations.summary_quote: The audit team identified a very high number of CSO partners that were directly selected or retained and also noted delays in signing partner agreements and in disbursing cash, as well as inconsistent use of eTools to upload partner progress reports for assessment of performance and programme results.

observations.title: Supply distribution and supply end-user monitoring
observations.summary_quote: The audit team noted inconsistent supply end-user monitoring, quality concerns, and a lack of follow-up of warehouse assessments. These issues heighten the risk of inefficiency, compromised supply quality, and potential service delivery gaps, which may erode beneficiary trust and reduce programme effectiveness.

observations.title: Protection from Sexual Exploitation and Abuse (PSEA)
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office’s risk management activities did not cover SEA-related risks and there was a lack of planning to respond to PSEA risks in emergency contexts. PSEA action plans were not consistently monitored or followed up, and there was a need to enhance PSEA measures with respect to high-risk partnerships.

agreed_actions.quote: Review and redesign, as necessary, the organizational structure and resource allocation for the performance and oversight of tasks currently assigned to the QA section, to achieve a more cost-effective, efficient solution that respects core principles of internal control and risk management

Improve current fraud risk management processes by ensuring inputs are received from all relevant staff and assess all relevant instances of possible fraud, corruption, and misconduct, including financial fraud, asset misappropriation, bribery, conflicts of interest, and unethical behaviour
Develop and implement measures to support partners in coordinating and consolidating cash requests to reduce the administrative workload for all parties.
Implement measures that ensure prompt completion of financial HACT assurance activities, to enable timely corrective measures.
Ensure that relevant staff are involved in the development of terms of reference for service contracts prior to advertisement of the request for proposals
Establish contingency Programme Documents for anticipated emergency programmes, to facilitate competitive partner selection and ensure preparedness.

Develop and implement a standardized approach to supply end-user monitoring, to ensure consistent application across all programmatic visits and systematic recording of observations, feedback, metrics and action points in eTools
Systematically assess, address and monitor PSEA risks for all vulnerable communities, as part of the Country Office's risk mitigation, emergency preparedness and response plans.

noteworthy_practices.quote: The Country Office structure and capacity were generally aligned with programmatic and operational needs, and a detailed accountability framework was collaboratively developed in 2021 to clarify roles and responsibilities.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Recommended Actions

noteworthy_practices.quote: Conditions in the UNICEF warehouse in Nairobi were found to be adequate and the logistics process was well managed.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Recommended Actions

title: 2024 OIAI Audit Report on the Lebanon Country Office

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management, or control processes were partially satisfactory, improvement needed, meaning that the assessed governance, risk management or control processes were generally adequate and functioning but needed improvement.

observations.title: Findings of HACT assurance activities
observations.summary_quote: The LCO did not always appropriately act on the financial findings from its HACT assurance activities, elevating the risk of financial losses that could result from fraud, waste and abuse of funds entrusted to partners.

observations.title: Cash assistance to beneficiaries
observations.summary_quote: There is a need to strengthen the management of risks related to payments under cash assistance programmes and grievance redressal mechanisms to increase their usefulness and ultimately help the LCO to appropriately address the needs of the target population.

observations.title: Payment of teachers’ salaries and operating expenses of schools
observations.summary_quote: There is a need to enhance controls around payment of salaries to teachers and to schools for operating expenses, as ghost workers or padded payrolls reduce the valuable resources meant for the most vulnerable.

observations.title: Programme visits and supply end-user monitoring
observations.summary_quote: As each programme section was responsible for executing their own programme visits, the country office did not have a comprehensive overview of any gaps or duplication in efforts or the adequacy of the overall coverage. Further, supplies end-user monitoring was not systematically performed to obtain assurance that supplies were indeed received by the intended beneficiaries.

observations.title: Programme reporting tools and databases
observations.summary_quote: Multiple unconnected systems may result in duplicative data entry points and a general lack of consistent and accurate information, which ensures everyone is working with the same data.

observations.title: Protection from sexual exploitation and abuse (PSEA)
observations.summary_quote: Inadequate confirmation of subcontracted partners’ PSEA assessments, insufficient SEA monitoring and awareness building of communities served elevates the risk that SEA incidents may occur and go unaddressed.

observations.title: Screening of consultants and contractors
observations.summary_quote: Inadequate screening of individual consultants and contractors against UN consolidated sanctions list exposes the LCO to potential reputation risk.

agreed_actions.quote: Pursuit of all available actions for the audit findings on questioned costs, and request UNICEF Division of Financial and Administrative Management (DFAM) for write-off if deemed appropriate.
Adapting cash to beneficiary processes based on lessons learned, regarding incorrect recipient identification and/or trends from comp

The country office agrees to enhance controls around payment of salaries to teachers and for operating expenses to schools by specifically requiring third-party monitors to confirm the existence of personnel, accuracy of personnel lists and a review of the MEHE’s recruitment processes.

Establish comprehensive planning for the management of all programme visits, that ensures compliance with the minimum requirements, and is also risk informed by planned visits in the programme documents and findings from prior programme visits.

The country office agrees to ensure that a plan of action is developed to integrate and align multiple reporting tools and databases where possible and reconcile the reports with corporate systems as applicable.
Improving SEA monitoring by the PSEA coordinator, focal points and CSO partners by setting key performance indicators for monitoring.
The country office agrees to ensure screening of individual consultants and contractors against the United Nations consolidated sanctions list before they are recruited.

noteworthy_practices.quote: the LCO worked with the UNICEF Middle East and North Africa Regional Office (MENARO) and/or UNICEF headquarters to strengthen internal controls like cash assistance to beneficiaries, supply chain management, and the government managed warehouse.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

title: 2024 OIAI Audit Report on Construction Management
period_covered: September 2022 to June 2023 (audits of construction management in eight UNICEF country offices)
fieldwork: September 2022 to June 2023; headquarters follow-up October–November 2023

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management, or control processes were Partially Satisfactory, Improvement Needed, meaning they were generally adequate and functioning, but needed some improvement.

observations.title: Planning of construction activities
observations.summary_quote: At the country office level, there was a need for enhanced guidance on the development of a robust business case for each construction project, to mitigate the risk of undertaking construction activities that do not materially contribute to programme goals.

observations.title: In-house capacity to manage construction projects
observations.summary_quote: Country offices with significant construction portfolios struggled to ensure adequate in-house expertise and capacity, which contributed to gaps in management and oversight of projects and significant delays.

observations.title: Visibility of indirect construction projects
observations.summary_quote: There was a lack of information on the total extent and value of construction projects funded by UNICEF due to the absence of data on construction implemented indirectly through partners.

observations.title: Procurement and contracting
observations.summary_quote: The audits identified some weaknesses in construction-related procurement and contracting in individual country offices, indicating inconsistent application of the relevant procedures and guidance. The findings did not indicate any systemic problems affecting the wider organization

observations.title: Construction monitoring
observations.summary_quote: While some degree of construction monitoring was observed in all the audits, gaps were observed in the execution, documentation and follow-up of monitoring activities in several country offices.

observations.title: Indemnification tools
observations.summary_quote: The country office audits indicated a good understanding of the importance of key financial instruments to manage risks related to construction supplier performance.

observations.title: Sustainability of constructed facilities
observations.summary_quote: The country office audits highlighted risks related to the maintenance and sustainability of construction outputs once handed over to the local authorities and/or beneficiaries.

agreed_actions.quote: Conduct a review, to determine the strategic direction for UNICEF’s construction activities and articulate the necessary support to Country Offices

revise the current procedures to provide flexible, principle-based guidance on the required capacity to manage and oversee construction projects, enabling the country offices to tailor resourcing to the specific context and donor conditionalities.
establish a system or mechanism to capture reliable, real-time data on the extent and value of indirectly implemented projects needed for effective oversight and project management.
assess the effectiveness and adequacy of the existing toolkit and provide comprehensive training to country offices to facilitate and ensure effective monitoring of construction projects.
develop and implement any additional tools and measures needed to mitigate the risk of poor supplier performance related to construction activities.
enhance guidance to country offices on construction sustainability and

noteworthy_practices.quote: Most of the country offices audited used a milestone-based payment system, whereby a supplier is paid only after achieving pre-established milestones, and none made advance payments to suppliers.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

noteworthy_practices.quote: Several country offices covered by the audit were collaborating with local authorities and beneficiary communities to ensure the long-term sustainability of facilities constructed by UNICEF.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

title: 2024 OIAI Audit Report of the Pakistan Country Office

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management or control processes were generally adequate and functioning but needed improvement. The weaknesses or deficiencies identified were unlikely to have a materially negative impact on the performance of the audited entity, area, activity or process.

observations.title: Staffing and oversight of work at provincial levels
observations.summary_quote: Delays in filling vacancies and lack of clear reporting lines between staff at the provincial levels and respective heads of the programme sections at Headquarters meant that the risks to the efficient and effectiveness implementation of programme activities at the provincial levels were not managed effectively.

observations.title: Contracts management
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office was making advance payments without obtaining the required prior approval from DFAM. Such approval involves additional due diligence that ensure effective management of the risk of fraud, waste and abuse associated with such payments.

observations.title: Cash transfers to government partners through single signatory accounts
observations.summary_quote: The cash transfers to some government partners were being made through banks accounts that were outside of the government financial management and accounting systems, which UNICEF relies on for the effective management of the transfers. There was thus the unmitigated risk of fraud, waste and abuse funds UNICEF transferred to those partners.

observations.title: Supply and Inventory management
observations.summary_quote: There were gaps in inventory management and the end-user monitoring processes, which reduced management’s oversight of and feedback on the movement of supplies once they were handed over to partners and after they were received by beneficiaries.

observations.title: Construction management
observations.summary_quote: At the time of the audit, there were 991 construction projects, implemented directly and through partners. There was a need for the Country Office to strengthen oversight over the planning, implementation and recording of all construction activities.

observations.title: Programme implementation monitoring
observations.summary_quote: Inadequate risk-based programme implementation monitoring and inconsistent third-party field monitoring impeded the Country Office’s ability to obtain necessary assurance that programme activities were implemented as planned and to assess the reliability of results reported by implementing partners.

observations.title: Prevention of Sexual Exploitation and Abuse (PSEA)
observations.summary_quote: Despite the development of a PSEA action plan and establishment of field office focal points, further efforts were required to strengthen the management of PSEA risk, including the performance and follow-up of PSEA assessments of civil society organization partners and human resources managed through third party service providers.

observations.title: Accountability to Affected Populations (AAP)
observations.summary_quote: UNICEF’s 2022-2025 AAP strategy requires all country offices to integrate AAP into their plans, with appropriate technical and financial support, and to ensure that country programmes are informed by the views, participation and feedback from communities.

agreed_actions.quote: Identify and take appropriate actions to minimize further delays in the recruitment process.
Obtain the requisite approval for advance payments and implement appropriate risk mitigation measures to manage its financial exposure to third party vendors

The Country Office agrees to take appropriate measures to effectively manage the risk of fraud, waste and abuse associated with funds UNICEF transferred to accounts that are not part of the Government financial management and accounting systems.
warehouse capacity assessments are conducted, as appropriate, for partners receiving supplies
Establishing a complete and comprehensive tracking mechanism that covers all construction activities
Putting in place appropriate processes to ensure adequate programme monitoring, including ensuring there is adequate quality, coverage and risk focus of monitoring activities
Ensure the necessary PSEA risk assessments and supporting action plans are undertaken and finalized within set timeframes for all service providers with direct access to targeted communities.
Develop and roll out an AAP action plan to ensure AAP activities and indicators are systematically included in programme and partnership documents

noteworthy_practices.quote: The Country Office conducted numerous anti-fraud workshops and activities and disseminated communications on fraud-related matters to staff, implementing partners, suppliers and vendors to raise awareness.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

noteworthy_practices.quote: Supply planning was timely and there was good collaboration between sections. The Country Office monitored the effective and efficient implementation of the supply plans during the audit period.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

title: 2024 OIAI Audit Report of Protection from Sexual Exploitation and Abuse

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management, or control processes were partially satisfactory, improvement needed, meaning that the assessed governance, risk management or control processes were generally adequate and functioning but needed improvement.

observations.title: Country office risk assessment and action planning
observations.summary_quote: There was a need for enhanced tools, guidance and training to support more robust assessment and management of SEA risks in the local context. The fragmented approach to global PSEA management and oversight increased the risk that country office action plans may not be aligned with country-specific risks and that any significant residual risk may go unnoticed and unaddressed.

observations.title: CSO partner and third-party SEA risk management
observations.summary_quote: There was a need to enhance processes, guidance and training to reinforce country offices’ ability to fulfil their responsibility to assess, build and monitor partner capacity to manage PSEA risk.

observations.title: Involvement of Government partners in PSEA
observations.summary_quote: UNICEF country offices do not require government partners to develop and implement actions for development of national PSEA systems, even though they implement community-based interventions, such as social cash transfers and distribution of supplies to beneficiaries in most UNICEF country offices.

observations.title: Community-based complaint mechanisms
observations.summary_quote: UNICEF is committed to reducing the level of under-reporting of SEA. Several Headquarters and inter-agency initiatives to address this were underway at the time of the audit. However, there was an opportunity to evaluate more systematically the reasons for the decrease in safe reporting channels available to communities

observations.title: Implementation of new Safeguarding (PSEA) policy
observations.summary_quote: The audit noted opportunities for improvement at the Headquarters level with respect to UNICEF’s global management of PSEA risk. These included reviewing and defining roles and accountabilities and improving cross-divisional collaboration

observations.title: Sustainable funding to implement PSEA strategy
observations.summary_quote: There was a need for a long-term sustainable fundraising plan to ensure successful implementation of UNICEF’s PSEA strategy.

observations.title: Monitoring progress in PSEA
observations.summary_quote: No formal evaluation of progress towards PSEA impacts and outcomes had been conducted globally or in any of the country offices reviewed by audit in the five years since the Independent Panel Review in 2018.

agreed_actions.quote: Establish a streamlined, holistic approach to SEA risk management and reporting, with a clear linkage between country office risk assessment and action planning, to facilitate the review and monitoring of the adequacy of mitigating actions to address context-specific risks
Review the various PSEA components of the CSI 3.5.4 to ensure that this indicator focuses on measuring country office efforts towards achievement of improved results for PSEA

agree to accelerate the establishment of measures to strengthen SEA risk management and capacity strengthening for government partners and issue guidance and provide relevant tools and capacity building on its application as needed.
accelerate the establishment of comprehensive community feedback mechanisms across all implementing partners and interventions and set up an indicator for community involvement in its local design

Determine the target organizational structure and skills required for effective implementation and monitoring of PSEA in Headquarters, regional offices and country offices and conduct a gap analysis to identify the resourcing strategy to achieve successful implementation
Determine a long-term funding strategy for PSEA, using the resourcing/skills gap analysis (see Action 5) and an analysis of the return on PSEA investment to date
agrees to establish a plan to ensure periodic evaluation of whether UNICEF’s PSEA efforts have accomplished the intended impact for leadership, staff, partners and local communities.

noteworthy_practices.quote: UNICEF’s Executive Director and senior leaders set the tone for an organizational culture in which no form of sexual exploitation or abuse is tolerated by UNICEF staff and partners.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

noteworthy_practices.quote: In 2023, UNICEF, along with other United Nations agencies, adopted a common methodology to assess the PSEA capacity of CSO partners and set up an online portal to facilitate partner selection and sharing of PSEA assessments and CSO action plans.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

title: 2024 OIAI Audit Report of UNICEF's Accountability to Affected Populations

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management or control processes were Partially Satisfactory, Improvement Needed, meaning that the weaknesses or deficiencies identified were unlikely to have a materially negative impact on the performance of the audited entity, area, activity or process.

observations.title: Leadership, accountability and responsibility
observations.summary_quote: Arrangements at the headquarter level for the leadership and management of AAP should be reviewed and adjusted as needed to strengthen the coordination and implementation of AAP strategic objectives and related roadmap.

observations.title: Feedback mechanisms
observations.summary_quote: Due to the lack of evidenced-based processes for obtaining feedback from beneficiaries, it was unclear how UNICEF’s requirement that all country programmes are informed by the views, participation and feedback from communities was being met.

observations.title: Integrating AAP into country plans and interventions
observations.summary_quote: Due to the lack of evidence-based processes for the consultation, collection and analysis of beneficiaries’ views and the use of such views to adjust programming, it was unclear how UNICEF’s requirement that all country programmes are informed by the views, participation and feedback from communities was being met.

observations.title: AAP in non-humanitarian contexts
observations.summary_quote: The UNICEF AAP handbook mandates the application of AAP in all programmes, humanitarian or development. The unclear authority and responsibility for guiding AAP implementation in development contexts may limit the ability of country offices to integrate AAP indicators in development programmes.

agreed_actions.quote: Reassess AAP governance structures and, based on the assessment, appropriately adjust and increase the scope for effective AAP.
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

agreed_actions.quote: Put in place appropriate mechanisms to support country offices in the collection of feedback.
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

agreed_actions.quote: Clarify how to integrate AAP in country programmes by defining clear roles and responsibilities for AAP at country level for the systematic integration of AAP in planning, monitoring and implementation processes
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

noteworthy_practices.quote: UNICEF developed a handbook, toolkits and online training to educate staff and implementing partners about AAP and guide them on the application of AAP principles in their daily work.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

title: 2024 OIAI Audit Report of Afghanistan Country Office

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management, or control processes were Partially Satisfactory, Major Improvement Needed meaning that the assessed governance, risk management or control processes needed major improvement.

observations.title: Senior Staff Turnover
observations.summary_quote: The country office has had higher than expected turnover at the representative level and key positions remained vacant at the time of the audit. This has had a negative effect on staff morale and if not addressed may affect programme delivery.

observations.title: Notes for the Record
observations.summary_quote: The country office processed several notes for the record, that were significant both in terms of value and deviation from procedure. Further, there was no formal tracking and storage method for hardcopy NFRs.

observations.title: Management of Implementing Partnerships
observations.summary_quote: While UNICEF’s preferred method of identifying implementing partners is open selection, UNICEF Afghanistan used direct selection and did not keep sufficient documentation on the selection process.

observations.title: Management of Extenders
observations.summary_quote: There were weaknesses in the management of extender contracts, including a lack of coordinated workforce planning, which resulted in lost discounts and not all extender staff attending mandatory PSEA training.

observations.title: HACT Assurance
observations.summary_quote: The country office should improve its approach to HACT programme assurance activities to maximize coverage of high-risk partners and make more efficient use of the results of both financial and programme assurance activities.

observations.title: Prevention of Sexual Exploitation and Abuse (PSEA)
observations.summary_quote: The safeguards to manage SEA risks by CSOs, which include conducting capacity assessments, undertaking skills strengthening activities, and developing a context-specific monitoring framework, were not operating effectively, thus increasing the risk of occurrence of SEA and related reputational risks to UNICEF.

observations.title: Warehousing and Supply End User Monitoring
observations.summary_quote: resulting in a lack of assurance as to the partners’ capacity to receive, store and distribute UNICEF supplies. In addition, there were gaps in supply end-user monitoring

observations.title: Safety and Security
observations.summary_quote: measures, including the security awareness training and advanced safe driving course to address emerging security risks, thus exposing staff to harm due to inadequately mitigated safety and security risks.

observations.title: Risk Management
observations.summary_quote: With respect to fraud risk management, there was a need to establish a procedure for escalating identified risks and red flags to ensure appropriate follow-up and investigation

agreed_actions.quote: The Regional Office for South Asia, in consultation with the Division of Human Resources and the country office, agrees to develop a plan to smooth transitions of senior leadership for identified critical positions in the ACO and reinforce the completion of their tour of duty in the country.
The country office agrees to ensure that all exceptions to UNICEF policies and procedures are managed in line with the relevant provisions of the UNICEF Policy on the Regulatory Framework.
Implement the competitive selection of CSOs, ensuring that rigorous, independent due diligence processes are applied on all new partners

Establish dedicated capacity for the planning, coordination, and oversight of the extender workforce to ensure that the number of service contracts for similar services are minimized, duplication of effort is limited and value for money is enhanced.

Improve the programmatic visit quality assurance process by assigning responsibilities for office-wide coordination and oversight of programmatic visits to ensure that the selection of implementing partners for programmatic visits is risk-based and the risk of working with high-risk partners is adequately mitigated.

Establish procedures to ensure that PSEA assessments/reassessments are promptly conducted, and that relevant capacity improvement plans are put in place for partners and the implementation of those plans is adequately tracked.

Ensure that capacity assessments are performed on implementing partners that receive significant or sensitive programme supplies and that supplies are dispatched based on an approved partner request and distribution plan designed to meet the needs of the communities served.

Undertake an assessment of the security needs of the office as part of UN-wide assessment, the capacity of the security service provider to meet these needs and update UNICEF private security contract accordingly.

establish an integrated risk management structure, supported by sufficient guidelines, tools and any necessary training, to ensure a coherent, efficient, comprehensive approach to the country office’s risk management
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

noteworthy_practices.quote: the United Nations Country Team (UNCT) agreed in November 2021 to work collectively towards a more coordinated risk management approach, and in April 2023, the Risk Management Team (RMT) was formalized and put in place to serve as an inter-agency platform for a common risk management approach.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

title: 2024 OIAI Audit Report of Ghana Country Office

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management, or control processes were Partially Satisfactory, Improvement Needed, meaning they were generally adequate and functioning, but needed some improvement.

observations.title: Fraud risk management
observations.summary_quote: While the Country Office had established measures to identify and mitigate fraud risks, there are opportunities to further strengthen its approach to preventing, detecting and response to fraud.

observations.title: Transfer and monitoring of programme supplies
observations.summary_quote: There was a need to clarify accountabilities and responsibilities for the management of supplies transferred to implementing partners and to expand the application of supply end-user monitoring. These and other weaknesses in supply and logistics management heightened the risk of diversion and theft of supplies.

observations.title: Construction contracts
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office needed to integrate budget and performance indicators for construction activities into the overall work planning process to facilitate monitoring and ensure achievement of programme results.

observations.title: HACT assurance activities
observations.summary_quote: The audit team noted several opportunities to strengthen the planning, execution and finalization of assurance activities. These included establishing office-wide coordination to maximize the coverage and usefulness of risk-based assurance activities; improving the timeliness of spot checks, spot-check reports and follow-up of assurance recommendations

observations.title: Protection from Sexual Exploitation and Abuse (PSEA)
observations.summary_quote: While the Country Office assessed partners’ SEA risks and established mitigating action plans, there was a need to integrate the follow-up of the partners’ agreed actions into field monitoring and to increase SEA awareness among partners and service providers working directly with the Country Office.

observations.title: Payment processing
observations.summary_quote: A significant proportion of payments to implementing partners and vendors were processed manually, exposing the Country Office to an increased risk of payments to unauthorized individuals or for unauthorized transactions.

agreed_actions.quote: Conduct a periodic assessment of the potential for fraud and adequacy of existing mitigating measures in all relevant activities (e.g., low-value procurement, manual payments and supply distribution) with consideration of all possible forms of fraud, corruption and misconduct

Map out the distribution and monitoring processes related to Health & Nutrition supplies, to accurately reflect the roles and responsibilities of all parties, identify key risks and assess the adequacy of control measures to mitigate identified risks
Establish a process to ensure the timely revision and alignment of work plans, budget and the RAM to capture activities not initially planned.
Establish a mechanism to ensure that spot checks are conducted and reports prepared in a timely manner.
Implement plans to integrate PSEA monitoring activities in the Country Office’s overall programme monitoring to ensure that action plans are adequately implemented to mitigate partners’ SEA risks.
The Country Office agrees to work with GSSC and the Regional Office to reduce the number of payments processed manually, and ensure that they are used only in exceptional circumstances.

noteworthy_practices.quote: The Country Office structure and capacity were generally aligned with programmatic and operational needs and a detailed accountability framework was developed to clarify staff roles and responsibilities in the Country Office and its field office.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

noteworthy_practices.quote: The Country Office was piloting a sanitation fund whereby small-scale loans were granted to households and Micro-, Small and Medium-sized Enterprises in selected municipal and metropolitan districts for the construction of latrines.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

title: 2024 OIAI Audit Report of UNICEF's Institutional Service Contracts

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management and control processes were Partially Satisfactory, Improvement Needed, meaning they were generally adequate and functioning, but needed some improvement.

observations.title: Vendor registration and evaluation
observations.summary_quote: The audit identified the need to enhance the efficiency and effectiveness of vendor registration and evaluation processes to mitigate the risk of awarding contracts to vendors that are not financially or ethically sound.

observations.title: Vendor review and sanctioning
observations.summary_quote: The audit identified the need to clarify and strengthen the vendor review and sanctioning processes to better mitigate risks that UNICEF may continue to engage with vendors that pose financial or integrity risks, including possible involvement in proscribed practices

observations.title: Contract Review Committee
observations.summary_quote: The Contract Review Committee (CRC) is a key control mechanism to ensure independent scrutiny of higher value service contracts prior to acceptance by the office head.

observations.title: Oversight and monitoring
observations.summary_quote: Different interpretations of headquarters and regional office accountabilities impacted the strength of oversight over UNICEF’s institutional service contracting.

observations.title: Vendor performance management and contract closure
observations.summary_quote: While a new application had recently been launched to digitize vendor performance evaluations and contract closure, some gaps in monitoring controls still needed to be addressed to ensure timely vendor performance evaluations and contract closure.

observations.title: Conflicts of interest and financial disclosure
observations.summary_quote: UNICEF regulations widely acknowledge the risk of conflict of interest in procurement of service contracting. The audit found room for improvement in administration of regulations designed to help manage the risks of conflicts of interest and in monitoring adherence to ensure effective control.

observations.title: Long-term arrangements
observations.summary_quote: The audit found inadequate monitoring of vendors providing services through long-term arrangements (LTAs), and limited strategic analysis of the use of LTAs, which increased the risk that efficiency and price benefits offered by such arrangements may not be fully exploited.

observations.title: Global Centre of Excellence
observations.summary_quote: In view of wider organizational changes, there was a need to review the ongoing validity of the goal of establishing a global service contracts Centre of Excellence, and to assess the effectiveness of the contracting category management units.

agreed_actions.quote: Assess the value of mandatory UNGM registration as part of the review of the procedure on Vendor Registration and Management, including identifying any integrity risks that are not covered by the UNGM
Review and revise UNICEF’s procedures and SOP related to the Vendor Review Committee, in light of issues identified in this audit
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

agreed_actions.quote: Evaluate the feasibility and cost/benefit of configuring eCRC to interface with VISION, for greater efficiency and improved internal controls and monitoring
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

agreed_actions.quote: Clarify and communicate accountabilities and responsibilities for monitoring headquarters, regional office and country office adherence to all mandatory procedures for institutional service contracts
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

agreed_actions.quote: Review and revise guidance and InSight metrics on contract and budget management in light of the new procurement case manager (PCM) application
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

agreed_actions.quote: Develop and communicate guidance on the type and level of organizational risks that should be considered by heads of offices in the identification of staff for participation in the Conflict of Interest Financial Disclosure Programme
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

agreed_actions.quote: Introduce a periodic strategic review of the creation and use of long-term arrangements (LTAs)
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

agreed_actions.quote: Review the ongoing validity of the decision to create a Global Services Centre of Excellence, using appropriate benchmarking, learnings from this audit and other assessments and a robust risk analysis
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

noteworthy_practices.quote: in 2021, the Supply Division’s Services Contracting Centre (SCC) began roll-out of a procurement case manager (PCM) application developed to improve the efficiency and visibility of workflows for Headquarters offices’ service contracting activities.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

title: 2024 OIAI Audit Report of the Egypt Country Office

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management, or control processes were Partially Satisfactory, Improvement Needed, meaning that the weaknesses or deficiencies identified were unlikely to have a materially negative impact on the performance of the audited entity, area, activity, or process.

observations.title: [Redacted observation]
observations.locator: Observation 1 (redacted per Executive Board decision EB2012/13)

observations.title: Programme planning
observations.summary_quote: There are opportunities to improve programme planning by (a) assessing CSOs subcontracted by the government for SEA and fraud risks as a part of the overall programme planning framework

observations.title: Programme monitoring
observations.summary_quote: Monitoring of programme activities and programme results was not sufficient to enable the CO to measure the impact of the programme which could lead to suboptimal outcomes for beneficiaries.

observations.title: Cash transfers to implementing partners
observations.summary_quote: Macro-assessment of public financial system risks was not conducted, and the use of alternate payees was not adequately documented to establish proof of relationship with the implementing partners.

observations.title: Financial assurance activities
observations.summary_quote: Insufficient financial assurance activities and inconsistent follow-up actions could hinder the CO's ability to provide assurance regarding the accuracy of financial information reported and that funds transferred to implementing partners are used as intended to achieve results for children.

observations.title: Supply management
observations.summary_quote: Inadequate planning and monitoring of supply plans, lack of warehouse assessments for CSOs, insufficient Contract Review Committee (CRC) training, and other issues pertaining to failure to close open purchase orders (POs) and conduct vendor evaluations could compromise the efficiency of UNICEF’s procurement activities, potentially leading to supply loss or damage.

agreed_actions.quote: The Egypt country office should assess CSOs subcontracted by the government for capacity and risk as a part of the overall programme planning framework, by ensuring that required SEA and fraud risk capacity assessments are carried out for these organizations
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of observations and agreed actions

agreed_actions.quote: implement robust risk-based field monitoring plans to ensure appropriate coverage and adequate frequency of programme monitoring visits
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of observations and agreed actions

agreed_actions.quote: strengthen its cash transfers process to implementing partners by documenting proof of relationship between the implementing partner and permitted payee
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of observations and agreed actions

agreed_actions.quote: enhance oversight of financial assurance activities by ensuring timely and adequately covered HACT assurance activities
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of observations and agreed actions

agreed_actions.quote: should monitor the implementation of supply plans, analyse discrepancies, update the supply plans on an ongoing basis
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of observations and agreed actions

noteworthy_practices.quote: OIAI noted the positive measures taken by the Egypt Country Office to enhance its partnership with other UN agencies through joint agreements and active participation in UN working groups.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of observations and agreed actions

title: 2024 OIAI Audit Report of the Mauritania Country Office

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management, or control processes were Partially Satisfactory, Improvement Needed, meaning that the weaknesses or deficiencies were unlikely to have a materially negative impact on the performance of the audited entity, area, activity or process.

observations.title: Fraud risk management
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office conducted an annual risk assessment, including fraud risk assessment, and developed an anti-fraud strategy. It needs to provide fraud training to implementing partners and service providers to increase their fraud risk awareness and reinforce UNICEF’s zero-tolerance policy on fraud.

observations.title: Recruitment
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office had developed and implemented its diversity, equity and inclusion strategy to address its human resources issues. It still needed to finalize the recruitment of the Chief, Education Programme to ensure adequate leadership and capacity to mobilize resources required to achieve the programmatic goals.

observations.title: Supply planning, inventory management and supply end-user monitoring (SEUM)
observations.summary_quote: Challenges in logistics and warehouse management reduced management’s oversight of the storage and movement of goods and the degree of assurance that supplies were being received on a timely basis by the intended beneficiaries.

observations.title: Accountability to Affected Populations (AAP)
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office did not develop an AAP strategy. Despite some efforts from Country Office programme sections to include an AAP approach in their activities, it was not consistently done across the Country Office.

observations.title: Safety and Security
observations.summary_quote: Weaknesses in the implementation of the programme criticality plan and in the office’s security assessments exposed the Country Office’s staff and programme activities to significant security risks given the country's context.

observations.title: Contracts for Services
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office needed to strengthen its mitigation of procurement and contract management risks, ensuring that staff involved have sufficient knowledge of the process and their roles and responsibilities.

observations.title: Protection from Sexual Exploitation and Abuse (PSEA)
observations.summary_quote: The Mauritania country context, including the humanitarian refugee crisis, has heightened the risk of Sexual Exploitation and Abuse (SEA).

observations.title: Social Cash Transfers
observations.summary_quote: The Grievance Committee was not monitoring the timeliness of responses to complainants. The Country Office has not assessed whether grievance mechanisms function as intended and meet beneficiaries’ needs.

observations.title: HACT Assurance Activities
observations.summary_quote: The implementation and oversight of HACT assurance activities were not adequate. Quality assurance over programmatic visit reports needed strengthening.

agreed_actions.quote: The Country Office should conduct fraud training for implementing partners and service providers to increase risk awareness and reinforce UNICEF’s zero-tolerance policy on fraud
Ensure that a signed agreement is established with the Government of Mauritania concerning its use of the Nouakchott warehouse, in which the roles and responsibilities of all parties are documented.

The Country Office should develop and roll out an Accountability to Affected Populations (AAP) strategy and action plan that is aligned with the strategic objectives of the UNICEF 2022-2025 AAP Strategy
In collaboration with the United Nations Country Team, work to ensure the implementation of programme criticality assessment in the humanitarian region.

Strengthen the quality assurance review over contracting to ensure that the legally binding agreement between UNICEF and a vendor is recorded, contracts are signed before their start date, and contracts are retained as per supply procedures.
Conclude the PSEA training for all implementing partners, service providers, focal points, and other key stakeholders

Assess the effectiveness of grievance mechanisms to ensure they function as intended, meet the beneficiaries' needs, and strengthen the sensibilization of beneficiaries to increase their knowledge of the available complaint mechanisms.
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

agreed_actions.quote: Strengthen implementation and oversight of HACT assurance activities to ensure that the assurance plan maximizes coverage of higher-risk partners and activities and complies with relevant corporate policies.

noteworthy_practices.quote: The Country Office developed and implemented a resource mobilization strategy. It included an action plan with assigned responsibilities and deadlines.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

noteworthy_practices.quote: The Country Office developed and implemented a Diversity, Equity and Inclusion (DEI) strategy that demonstrated improvement in key indicators related to geographic and gender balance.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

title: 2024 OIAI Audit Report of the Türkiye Country Office

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management, or control processes were Partially Satisfactory, Improvement Needed, meaning that the assessed governance, risk management or control processes were generally adequate and functioning but needed improvement.

observations.title: Partnerships Management
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office’s response to the 2023 earthquakes alerted it to the need for more robust contingency planning with partners. There was also a need for more coordinated monitoring with other United Nations agencies to ensure that, in cost-sharing situations, UNICEF is only charged for work completed on its behalf.

observations.title: Procurement, Management and Distribution of Supplies
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office did not conduct warehouse capacity assessments for partners. Therefore, it could not be determined whether partners had the capacity to receive, store, and distribute supplies sent to them by UNICEF.

observations.title: Local Programme and Monitoring Systems
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office used generic agreement templates to contract with cloud vendors, increasing the risk that when the Country Office moves to the Partner Reporting Portal, vendors may retain UNICEF’s data stored with the vendor or in the cloud.

observations.title: Programme Monitoring
observations.summary_quote: There were weaknesses in the design, implementation and monitoring of programmatic visits by the Country Office. The weaknesses included fewer than expected visits and programmatic visit reports.

observations.title: Supply End-user Monitoring (SEUM)
observations.summary_quote: Several issues with supply end-user monitoring practices at the Country Office increased the risk of unused, wasted and misappropriated supplies.

observations.title: Humanitarian Cash Transfers
observations.summary_quote: Weaknesses were noted in the Country Office’s oversight of the implementing partner that conducted the humanitarian cash transfer programme.

observations.title: Procurement and Management of Service Contracts
observations.summary_quote: The selection and evaluation of vendor bids, contract awards, and contract management processes for service contracts were generally satisfactory. However, the Country Office did not close contracts promptly after project completion, resulting in open balances that could not be used for other purposes.

agreed_actions.quote: Existing and future Programme Documents signed with partners with humanitarian capacity are updated to include contingency clauses up to 10 per cent of the budget to support emergency response.
Capacities and capabilities of partners are assessed before entering into storage and distribution agreements.
Implement the corporate tools (the Field Monitoring Module and Partner Reporting Portal) by January and December 2025, respectively, as planned.
The Country Office agrees to develop a monitoring framework to ensure that: i. Programmatic visits are conducted to the appropriate standards.
Supply end-user monitoring visits are completed as required.

Assess the risks of using the single CSO partner to perform multiple functions related to the ‘Children First’ programme and modify its processes to minimize risk and support adequate segregation of duties concerning cash transfers to beneficiaries.
Supply and Logistics monitor the closure of purchase orders and escalate unresolved issues to senior Management.

noteworthy_practices.quote: The Country Office established multi-sectoral simplified programme documents (SPDs) to cover education and child protection, making it easier for programme sections to manage the partnership and monitor the partner’s programme interventions.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

noteworthy_practices.quote: The Country Office embedded PSEA indicators in rolling work plans for four government partner municipalities to ensure that SEA risk is adequately addressed.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

title: 2025 OIAI Audit Report of Albania Country Office

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management, or control processes were Partially Satisfactory, Improvement Needed meaning that the weaknesses or deficiencies identified were unlikely to have a materially negative impact on the performance of the audited entity, area, activity, or process

observations.title: Programmatic monitoring
observations.summary_quote: Quality assurance over programmatic visit reports needs strengthening. Recommendations from programmatic visits were not systematically recorded to facilitate tracking and follow-up of action points, potentially impacting the delivery of results.

observations.title: Prevention of sexual exploitation and abuse
observations.summary_quote: Documentation of PSEA considerations in programmatic visits needs strenthening. There was no documented evidence of PSEA considerations in programmatic visits which may result in limited visibility on compliance to PSEA indicators by partners. SEA/SH risks were also not documented in the ACO’s annual risk assessment.

observations.title: Service contract management
observations.summary_quote: The ACO did not maintain an updated procurement plan to facilitate effective monitoring, tracking and transparency of service contracts throughout the year. Declarations of impartiality to confirm absence of conflict of interest were not consistently obtained during technical evaluations.

agreed_actions.quote: Develop and implement an offline monitoring checklist/template for programmatic visits, compliant with eTools FMM module
Update programmatic visit template to include SEA/SH-related questions as per UNICEF monitoring procedures
Regularly monitor and update the procurement plan to address ad hoc and emerging needs

noteworthy_practices.quote: In 2024, the ACO engaged 13 private sector partners and raised US$100,000 in funds through the digital platform of a private partner.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of observations and agreed actions

title: 2025 OIAI Audit Report of Algeria Country Office

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management, or control processes were generally adequate and functioning but needed improvement. The weaknesses or deficiencies identified were unlikely to have a materially negative impact on the performance of the audited entity, area, activity or process.

observations.title: Risk management
observations.summary_quote: The Algeria Country Office's risk management process could be improved by ensuring key risks affecting programmatic results for children are identified and addressed, and by ensuring the efficiency of controls to manage them.

observations.title: Result-based planning and monitoring
observations.summary_quote: Timely signing of workplans, clear goals, and robust monitoring can significantly enhance ACO programme implementation and effective monitoring, ensuring complete delivery and impactful results.

observations.title: Partnership Management
observations.summary_quote: Effective formalization and streamlined processes can foster strong partnerships, enhancing the overall results for children.

observations.title: Humanitarian Response
observations.summary_quote: Limited coordination and knowledge sharing in the humanitarian context, combined with the absence of AAP and PSEA activities, indicators, and budget, reduce operational collaboration and could result in duplicated efforts, inefficiencies, and misalignment of interventions.

observations.title: Donor Reporting and Fund Management
observations.summary_quote: Challenges such as earmarked funding, late receipt of donor funds, and fixed expiration dates can impact the achievement of programme results.

observations.title: Cash Transfers and Financial Assurance
observations.summary_quote: Limited adoption of the HACT framework due to operational considerations and the corporate configuration, combined with inadequate risk management and assurance measures for cash to beneficiaries could reduce the office's ability to ensure that planned activities are implemented as intended and that cash reaches the targeted beneficiaries.

observations.title: Procurement Management
observations.summary_quote: The absence of a market survey and the occurrence of late and inadequately planned procurement of goods and services may lead to less informed procurement decisions, potentially resulting in inefficiencies.

agreed_actions.quote: maintain regular risk management consultations with the risk focal point to discuss risk management strategies, ensure input and technical guidance received are reflected in the updated risk register
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Agreed Actions & Audit Ratings

agreed_actions.quote: The Algeria Country Office agreed to finalize and validate the 2025–2026 workplans in a timely manner.
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Agreed Actions & Audit Ratings

agreed_actions.quote: enhance the quality and consistency of programme documents by reinforcing internal quality assurance processes to ensure activities and budgets are clearly defined.
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Agreed Actions & Audit Ratings

agreed_actions.quote: systematically exchanging documents and practices through existing coordination mechanisms. Document these exchanges through joint meeting minutes and shared tools
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Agreed Actions & Audit Ratings

agreed_actions.quote: Consolidate donor reporting workflows with office-wide trackers, structured timelines, and clear staff responsibilities.
Implement the standard operating procedures for targeting beneficiaries to properly document FACE forms and itemized cost estimates
Finalize the market survey and use its findings to expand its supplier base and strengthen future procurement decisions

noteworthy_practices.quote: ACO conducted an annual review with key government counterparts to assess progress towards achieving expected results.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

noteworthy_practices.quote: A field office was created for the first time in Tindouf, and the Sahrawi Refugee Response Plan (SRRP) 2024-2025 was developed in close collaboration with all partners.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

title: 2025 OIAI Audit Report of the Libya Country Office

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management, or control processes were Partially Satisfactory, Improvement Needed, meaning that the assessed governance, risk management or control processes were generally adequate and functioning but needed improvement.

observations.title: Fraud risk management
observations.summary_quote: There were opportunities to strengthen the Country Office’s capacity to prevent, detect and respond to fraud. These included assessing all activities susceptible to fraud, identifying the need for additional mitigating actions, and monitoring and updating the risk register on a regular basis.

observations.title: Partnership management and cash transfers
observations.summary_quote: Delays in processing FACE forms resulted in late cash disbursement to implementing partners, in some cases close to or after grant expiry, increasing the risk of delayed programme activities.

observations.title: Field monitoring
observations.summary_quote: There were weaknesses in the design, implementation and monitoring of programmatic visits by the Country Office, in particular related to humanitarian emergencies and protection from sexual exploitation and abuse.

observations.title: Management of construction activities
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office did not have adequate mechanisms in place to ensure sufficient oversight and mitigate the risks specific to construction activities, including regular project tracking and performance management of consultant engineers.

observations.title: Supply planning, distribution and supply end-user monitoring
observations.summary_quote: While the Country Office established supply and procurement plans, a general lack of systematic supply plan monitoring contributed to last-minute procurement, distribution delays and demurrage costs.

observations.title: Procurement management
observations.summary_quote: The audit team noted areas for improvement in the Country Office’s application of required procurement controls, which indicated a need to provide relevant training for Contract Review Committee members and other staff involved in the procurement process.

agreed_actions.quote: Strengthen staff capacity in fraud risk management, seeking assistance from the Regional Office and UNICEF’s Chief Risk Officer, as appropriate

Review and revise, as appropriate, the contribution percentages required from partners, record all contributions (including in-kind contributions, such as provision of warehousing facilities) and monitor adherence to the agreed limits
Complete the recruitment of community-based facilitators, as planned, in order to perform adequate monitoring in Sudanese migrant areas.
nhance management of construction activities by implementing a structured and timely tracking system
Establish a mechanism to strengthen supply planning by timely approval, systematic updates, and alignment of procurement with programme needs and available grants
stablish a process for timely follow-up and payment of vendor invoices, closure of completed purchase orders and evaluation of vendors’ performance

noteworthy_practices.quote: despite the need for staff cuts in many areas, the Country Office retained key Finance and Supply positions, to ensure continued functioning of essential controls over the significant cash transfers and supply contributions to implementing partners in the complex country context.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

noteworthy_practices.quote: The Country Office had proactively established 15 new LTAs in 2024 and early 2025 to accelerate procurement of supplies in emergency situations.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

title: 2025 OIAI Audit Report of Lesotho Country Office

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management, or control processes were Partially Satisfactory, Major Improvement Needed, meaning that weaknesses or deficiencies identified could have a materially negative impact on the performance of the audited entity, area, activity or process

observations.title: Financial management and oversight
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office did not periodically analyse its financial statements to identify and monitor trends or anomalies requiring further investigation and potential mitigating action.

observations.title: Risk management including fraud risk management
observations.summary_quote: Several key risks were not captured in the Country Office’s risk register, which was not used by management to support the regular review and oversight of key risks and the adequacy of mitigating measures.

observations.title: Protection from sexual exploitation and abuse (PSEA)
observations.summary_quote: The PSEA reassessment for low or medium capacity CSO partners was overdue and low PSEA capacity partners did not have any PSEA programming activity in the signed programme documents.

observations.title: Cash Transfer to Implementing Partners
observations.summary_quote: Implementation of some programme activities continued beyond grant expiry due to insufficient monitoring and oversight of FACE forms issued close to grant expiry, and lack of awareness by new programme staff regarding UNICEF procedure on grants management.

observations.title: HACT assurance activities
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office did not obtain sufficient assurance on the use of funds transferred to implementing partners to mitigate the risk of undetected fraud, wastage and misuse of UNICEF resources.

observations.title: Programme Monitoring
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office did not have an office-wide monitoring plan but relied on each section to conduct individually managed programme monitoring visits. This uncoordinated approach led to inefficiencies, with multiple trips conducted by different sectors to the same partners/sites.

observations.title: Supply and Service Contracts
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office did not exercise oversight of procurement planning and implementation, which led to last-minute procurement requests that resulted in incorrect application of the low-value procurement rules.

observations.title: Travel Administration
observations.summary_quote: There was insufficient evidence that all travel expenditures were linked to activities in the annual workplans, thereby increasing the risk of unplanned travel that does not contribute to the achievement of programmatic results.

agreed_actions.quote: Conduct periodic analysis and monitoring of financial trends and ratios, and assign clear responsibilities for ensuring that necessary actions are taken to address any identified risk areas or issues which need to be escalated to the Regional Office.

Conduct a comprehensive fraud risk assessment which adequately reflects the operating environment, and the fraud risks specific to different activities, as well as the adequacy of mitigating controls, for inclusion in the Country Office risk register
Develop and implement a training plan to strengthen staff capacity in PSEA, with the support of the Regional Office
Enhance oversight of the timeliness of processing FACE forms (including direct payments and reimbursements) and create a mechanism to track FACE forms from submission date to date of system input
Identify the specific reasons for the government partners' lack of cooperation and collaborate with them in addressing the concerns to facilitate the performance of the required assurance activities
Develop and implement a monitoring strategy that adopts a risk-based approach
Adopt the Procurement Case Manager (PCM) to automate the management of institutional service contracts and regularly monitor and update the procurement plan

noteworthy_practices.quote: In October 2024, the Country Office established monthly ‘housekeeping’ meetings, chaired by the Deputy Representative Programmes and attended by Programme staff from all sections and Operations staff.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

noteworthy_practices.quote: In response to the results of the 2024 Global Staff Survey (GSS) in November 2024, the Country Office Representative held two consultative meetings with staff, followed by an online survey, inviting all staff to select one priority area and proposed action to address the topics with the lowest scores in the GSS.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

title: 2025 OIAI Audit Report on the Republic of Zambia Country Office

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management, or control processes were Partially Satisfactory, Improvement Needed meaning that governance, risk management, and control processes were generally adequate and functioning but needed improvement

observations.title: Risk Management
observations.summary_quote: The absence of a structured risk assessment process for high-risk activities or areas susceptible to fraud hampered the ability of the ZCO to proactively detect and respond to the risk of fraud, particularly in the operating context of fund flow through devolved government structures with insufficient capacity for oversight by line ministries.

observations.title: Work planning
observations.summary_quote: The absence of signed workplans as formal legal instruments with cash transfer recipients or insufficient detailed workplans with government partners has resulted in risks related to accountability, programme alignment, and financial oversight.

observations.title: Programme Monitoring
observations.summary_quote: Gaps were noted in tracking supply distribution, aligning monitoring with actual implementation, and there was no finalized structure reflecting the office-wide monitoring framework, impeding the effectiveness of oversight over programme implementation and reporting.
