observations.title: Management of partners’ fiduciary risk – HACT
observations.summary_quote: There was insufficient ownership of HACT and assurance processes, and recurrent procedural errors indicated gaps in compliance with the UNICEF HACT framework.

observations.title: Supply and Logistics Management
observations.summary_quote: Insufficient supply needs and logistics capacity assessment led to wastage, increased logistics costs and missed opportunities to support intended beneficiaries due to expired programme supplies.

observations.title: Travel Management
observations.summary_quote: There were lapses in travel management and insufficient monitoring, which exposed the ZCO to financial and other operational risks.

agreed_actions.quote: Conduct a fraud risk assessment based on global guidance or a locally developed methodology to identify and address control weaknesses

Develop a modified version of the annual workplans, by exporting from eWP and manually adding in the details of districts and subnational divisions, before signature at national level by line ministries
Prepare supply monitoring plans based on actual distributions, including reports on unused supplies.
Implement the 2025 spot check guidelines including for thresholds for financial findings requiring follow-up
ZCO agrees to conduct formal capacity assessment of partners before entering into storage or distribution agreements
Establish a travel management SOP clarifying accountabilities and responsibilities

title: 2025 OIAI Report on the Middle East and North Africa Regional Office

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management or control processes were Partially Satisfactory, Improvement Needed, meaning that governance, risk management and control processes were generally adequate and functioning but needed improvement

observations.title: Regional Office Accountabilities
observations.summary_quote: While some elements of MENARO’s key accountabilities were clearly defined by the UNICEF Accountability Framework, there was ambiguity around how mutual accountability for regional programme interventions, partnerships and operations was to be discharged
observations.locator: Observation — Regional Office Accountabilities (recommendations addressed to Headquarters, rated ‘High Priority’)

observations.title: Risk Management
observations.summary_quote: There was a need to enhance MENARO’s oversight and support of regional and country office risk management activities, including the quality assurance of country office risk registers, identification and review of emerging risks, timely escalation of risks and determination of regional risk appetite.

observations.title: Programmatic and Operational Support and Oversight
observations.summary_quote: While MENARO supported the roll-out of many corporate policies, procedures, guidance and tools, its ability to monitor country office compliance with some policies was limited by a misalignment between its accountabilities and the related authority.

observations.title: Regional Office Performance Metrics
observations.summary_quote: In the absence of corporate guidance on the measurement of regional office performance, there was room for improvement in how MENARO’s overall performance across sections was planned, assessed and monitored.

observations.title: Partnership and Resource Mobilization
observations.summary_quote: There was a need to better capture and measure the contribution of the Partnerships and Resource Mobilization function towards improved fundraising results across the MENA region.

observations.title: People and Culture
observations.summary_quote: There was a need to review the role of MENARO in a number of HR-related processes (e.g., recruitment, surge capacity) and the availability of skills to support the specific regional operating context.

observations.title: Knowledge Management and Sharing
observations.summary_quote: There were opportunities for MENARO to further enhance its role in capturing and sharing knowledge for improved organizational performance across the offices in the region

observations.title: Protection from Sexual Exploitation and Abuse (PSEA)
observations.summary_quote: As PSEA is a critical UNICEF priority and in view of the L3 emergencies and protracted humanitarian crises in the region, there was a need to clarify the role of regional offices in supporting and strengthening PSEA implementation, quality assurance, and country office capacity-building

agreed_actions.quote: develop and implement a regional risk appetite statement and strengthen the identification and management of new/emerging and common regional risks, the quality assurance of country office risk registers and the application of risk escalation protocols.

Work with the Future Focus Initiative implementation team to develop a document that clarifies the role of the Regional Office and how this role is operationalized, in line with the revised business delivery model for UNICEF.
review, revise and develop, as needed, the performance indicators in the Regional Office Management Plan (ROMP) submission for 2026
Develop and monitor specific indicators that take into account the plans to achieve regional resource mobilization objectives and expected results
In collaboration with Headquarters divisions, expand the roster to establish a well-staffed regional surge capacity that can be supplemented by global capacity as and when needed
develop sub-regional Knowledge Management approaches by country typology with programmatic and operational similarities for a more targeted and cohesive knowledge exchange.
update the existing MENARO PSEA workplan to include the key PSEA interventions

noteworthy_practices.quote: Given the high number of recruitment cases across the MENA country offices, and the need to free up capacity in country offices for other HR activities, such as workforce planning, staff development and wellbeing, MENARO created a regional recruitment hub.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

noteworthy_practices.quote: As part of the lessons learned from PBRs, in 2024, MENARO started to request country office affordability and sustainability analyses along with the PBR submission at least once a year.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

noteworthy_practices.quote: MENARO piloted a new dashboard which incorporated additional implementing partner financial information to strengthen country offices’ financial assurance planning and risk management.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

title: 2025 OIAI Audit Report of Romania Country Office

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management, or control processes were Partially Satisfactory, Improvement Needed meaning that the assessed governance, risk management, and control processes were generally adequate and functioning but needed improvement.

observations.title: Country Office Sustainability and Risk Management
observations.summary_quote: The audit acknowledged the Country Office’s efforts to ensure a structured, well-managed transition to operate without regular resources by 2026 and to be self-funding by 2028 but noted that the recent global funding cuts and related decisions may create additional operational, programmatic and people-related risks requiring careful management.

observations.title: Protection from Sexual Exploitation and Abuse (PSEA)
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office had not updated its PSEA Action Plan since 2023 so it was unclear what targets the Country Office was planning to achieve in this area and how it would ensure adequate focus on PSEA activities.

observations.title: Harmonized Approach to Cash Transfers (HACT) Assurance
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office had not followed up expenditure identified as ineligible by third-party spot-check service providers to confirm its ineligibility and whether action was required to recover the expenditure.

observations.title: Programme Field Monitoring
observations.summary_quote: Action points from programmatic visit reports were not consistently recorded in eTools for tracking and closure, with the result that necessary improvements may not be systematically followed up and programme activities may not be implemented efficiently and effectively in line with agreed goals.

agreed_actions.quote: Establish a process to ensure that new and emerging risks from all relevant sources and associated mitigating actions are captured in the risk register frequently enough to ensure its reliability and relevance
Review and update its PSEA Action Plan at least annually, to ensure that it is aligned with UNICEF’s monitoring framework and defines key PSEA-related outputs and results to be achieved.
Introduce a mechanism to ensure that all potentially ineligible expenditure is followed up by the
The Country Office will ensure that all relevant action items from programmatic visit reports that were not captured in eTools are recorded in eTools for follow-up and closure.

noteworthy_practices.quote: The Country Office has developed and successfully implemented programmes that meet the needs of both vulnerable children in Romania and refugees impacted by the war in Ukraine.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

noteworthy_practices.quote: The Country Management Team was proactive in preparing a structured transition to high-income country status.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

title: 2025 OIAI Audit Report on the Democratic Republic of the Congo Country Office

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management or control processes were Unsatisfactory, meaning that the assessed governance, risk management or control processes were not adequately established or did not function well.

observations.title: Fraud Risk Management
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office did not fully assess fraud risks across all activities susceptible to fraud, thus limiting its capacity to mitigate those risks.

observations.title: Governance and Office Structure
observations.summary_quote: Although the Country Office had an accountability framework, a weak office culture and inadequate management oversight undermined the effectiveness of supervisory controls over programme activities and support operations.

observations.title: Management of Third-party Consultants
observations.summary_quote: While individual and institutional contract needs are assessed at the beginning of each year, the Country Office does not conduct a thorough human resource needs assessment and lacks a strategy for recruiting and managing consultants.

observations.title: Construction Contracts
observations.summary_quote: Some construction projects were not planned and executed on time within the grant validity period. There was a lack of information on the extent and value of indirect construction projects executed by implementing partners.

observations.title: Supply Planning
observations.summary_quote: the Country Office did not have an adequate process to regularly review and revise the approved Supply Plan to reflect changes in stock levels and needs. Supplies pre-positioned at partners’ warehouses were not considered when the Country Office developed its subsequent plans.

observations.title: Transfer and Monitoring of Programme Supplies
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office transferred supplies to implementing partners above approved supplies budgets established in the programme documents.

observations.title: Protection from Sexual Exploitation and Abuse (PSEA)
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office did not conduct a thorough PSEA risk assessment as part of its Country Office Risk Assessment. No action points were established for those identified risks. The Country Office did not consistently monitor PSEA during programmatic visits to implementing partners, resulting in significant gaps in this critical monitoring.

observations.title: Partnership Management
observations.summary_quote: There were delays in finalizing programme documents with Civil Society Organization (CSO) implementing partners and releasing funds to implement the activities assigned to them. The Country Office did not provide sufficient justification for the direct selection of partners.

observations.title: Cash Transfers to Implementing Partners
observations.summary_quote: Despite the high-risk ratings of implementing partners, outstanding cash transfers, as well as ineligible expenses, the Country Office continues to use direct cash transfers, thus increasing the risk of delays in liquidating cash and diversion of funds. Cash transfers of US$2.6 million were outstanding for over six months, with more than US$731,000 aged for over two years.

observations.title: Harmonized Approach to Cash Transfers (HACT) Assurance Activities
observations.summary_quote: In 2024, the Country Office did not complete spot checks for 40 partners who liquidated cash totalling US$9.7 million, and there were no programmatic visits for 43 partners who received cash transfers amounting to US$8.9 million and supplies worth US$7.9 million.

observations.title: Cash Assistance Programmes
observations.summary_quote: Beneficiaries’ mobile account numbers for one of the programmes were not verified and confirmed before making payments to them. Service providers distributing cash to beneficiaries lacked adequate liquidity to serve the affected communities, which resulted in beneficiaries paying a commission to local payment agents to ensure that they received their money in a timely manner.

observations.title: Procurement of Goods and Services
observations.summary_quote: Controls related to awarding contracts to vendors with existing long-term agreements (LTAs) were insufficient to prevent or detect fraud in the contract award process. This, combined with the lack of impartiality and confidentiality declarations by staff conducting tender evaluations, as well as gaps in the invoice certification process and contract closure, exposes the Country Office to the risk of fraud.

observations.title: Travel Management
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office did not have a travel plan that included the anticipated need for travel in line with its operations and programmes. The Country Office did not implement necessary controls or oversight to monitor staff travel days and associated costs.

observations.title: Asset Management
observations.summary_quote: Assets owned by the Country Office and valued at approximately US$2.4 million were either declared missing (951), damaged (303) or obsolete (18). The Country Office was unable to provide the auditors with documentation to support these declarations.

agreed_actions.quote: assess the risks and related controls in all areas susceptible to fraud and implement an action plan and appropriate management oversight mechanism to address gaps or significant control weaknesses
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Agreed Actions & Audit Ratings

agreed_actions.quote: establish a centralized framework for monitoring field office operations and other support functions
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Agreed Actions & Audit Ratings

agreed_actions.quote: Develop a strategy, including a needs assessment, concerning the recruitment of consultants and the delivery of their work
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Agreed Actions & Audit Ratings

agreed_actions.quote: Improve construction planning and oversight by ensuring that construction contracts are issued promptly, and that construction projects are monitored and completed within the grant validity periods
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Agreed Actions & Audit Ratings

agreed_actions.quote: Develop an agile process that requires the Supply Plan to be regularly reviewed and revised to reflect changes in stock levels and needs, appropriately approved by the Country Management Team
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Agreed Actions & Audit Ratings

agreed_actions.quote: Assess warehouse and logistics capacity of implementing partners receiving supplies from the Country Office, and confirm and document that they have sufficient and current insurance coverage before releasing supplies
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Agreed Actions & Audit Ratings

agreed_actions.quote: Ensure all necessary annual PSEA Risk Assessments are completed and include a thorough assessment of SEA risks and the design and implementation of SEA prevention controls
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Agreed Actions & Audit Ratings

agreed_actions.quote: Establish efficient and effective review processes that mandate the prompt completion of partnership agreements and release of funds to implementing partners
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Agreed Actions & Audit Ratings

agreed_actions.quote: Ensure that all implementing partners are trained on how to complete FACE forms, and enhance monitoring controls to ensure that the details are accurately recorded in the FACE forms
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Agreed Actions & Audit Ratings

agreed_actions.quote: Strengthen assurance activities by applying all elements of the HACT framework and strengthening oversight and coordination of the planning process to ensure it is based on a risk-based assurance model that considers partners’ risk rating
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Agreed Actions & Audit Ratings

agreed_actions.quote: Strengthen the process for validating health workers’ mobile numbers before rendering payment to them
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Agreed Actions & Audit Ratings

agreed_actions.quote: Strengthen and secure the contract award process by ensuring all communications concerning financial offers from vendors with existing Long-Term Agreements are exchanged only through the secure email
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Agreed Actions & Audit Ratings

agreed_actions.quote: Develop a travel plan that aligns with the Country Office travel needs and the field monitoring plan, and ensure that the plan is approved by senior management and Chiefs of Field Offices and periodically updated
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Agreed Actions & Audit Ratings

agreed_actions.quote: Recover the value of missing assets from the staff members responsible in accordance with UNICEF's procedure
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Agreed Actions & Audit Ratings

noteworthy_practices.quote: The DRC Country Office maintains a strong presence in Eastern DRC, where several emergencies occurred during the audit period.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

noteworthy_practices.quote: All five donors interviewed during the audit confirmed a strong and positive relationship with the DRC Country Office.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

title: 2025 OIAI Audit Report on the Philippines Country Office

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management, or control processes were Partially Satisfactory, Improvement Needed, meaning that the weaknesses or deficiencies identified were unlikely to have a materially negative impact on the performance of the audited entity, area, activity or process

observations.title: Risk/Fraud Risk Management
observations.summary_quote: While the Country Office had taken steps to enhance risk management, such as designating Risk Champions and conducting training, risk management was not fully embedded in day-to-day operations. Specific risks related to large or innovative projects were not systematically assessed, and fraud risks were not adequately assessed or integrated into risk management activities.

observations.title: Business Continuity Planning
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office’s Business Continuity Plan, last updated in 2022, was not aligned with current operational realities. Testing and staff awareness activities were not conducted, and several designated staff roles were outdated.

observations.title: Programme Implementation Oversight
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office had strengthened its oversight of programme implementation, for example, through section-level monitoring plans, standardized templates and partner support mechanisms. However, there was a need to improve the integration of programmatic and financial oversight

observations.title: Partnership Management
observations.summary_quote: While foundational controls were in place, delays in the finalization of Programme Documents (PDs) and Small-Scale PDs, as well as a backlog of overdue or unreviewed Quarterly Progress Reports (QPRs), weakened the timeliness and traceability of oversight activities.

observations.title: HACT Assurance Activities
observations.summary_quote: Despite strong engagement in assurance planning and third-party spot-checks, the quality of HACT assurance implementation was uneven. Some partners did not undergo all required spot-checks, and documentation often lacked signatures, classification or timely partner feedback.

observations.title: Travel Management
observations.summary_quote: Travel transactions were frequently misclassified in general ledger accounts, with more than 100 instances of non travel-related expenses (e.g., IT equipment, training) charged to travel codes. This distorted financial reporting, created compliance risks, and undermined the integrity of expenditure data used for management decision-making and external reporting.

observations.title: Vendor and Contract Management
observations.summary_quote: While the Country Office had established procedures for contract issuance and vendor oversight, the audit noted deficiencies in the timely closure of service contracts, alignment of contract validity with grant periods, and enforcement of risk mitigation measures in vendor engagements.

agreed_actions.quote: Embed structured risk analysis and regular monitoring of mitigating actions into high-value or decentralized initiatives and activities

Establish a structured review and update mechanism for the BCP, clearly assigning accountability and integrating the process into the Annual Management Plan, operational risk reviews, and other routine management cycles

The Country Office agrees to strengthen integration of programmatic and financial oversight by establishing a mechanism to regularly reconcile planned, reported and liquidated expenditure at partnership level, supported by improved FACE form review practices and targeted staff guidance.
Strengthen coordination across Programme and Operations teams to reduce delays in PD/SPD finalization and QPR reviews

Strengthen assurance planning and execution by improving collaboration between Programme and Finance teams, ensuring that all high-value partners are reviewed through both system-generated and risk-informed criteria
Broaden verification processes across all expenditure types to ensure correct GL coding, including through targeted checks during TA, FACE form, and payment request reviews
Clarify and reinforce roles and timelines for contract closure, vendor evaluation, and grant alignment, supported by joint programme, supply, and finance oversight

noteworthy_practices.quote: In Valenzuela City, the child protection model was integrated into city governance and budgeting frameworks, strengthening institutional sustainability and reducing parallel implementation.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

noteworthy_practices.quote: The consistent physical presence of UNICEF staff in conflict-affected and hard-to-reach areas, including BARMM, was recognized by partners as a critical enabler of programme delivery and trust.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

noteworthy_practices.quote: The audit observed an internal culture of teamwork and adaptability among the Country Office’s staff, enabling effective external engagement and coordinated multi-sector delivery.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

title: 2025 OIAI Audit Report on the Cameroon Country Office

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management, or control processes were partially satisfactory, major improvement needed, meaning that the assessed governance, risk management, or control processes needed major improvement.

observations.title: Resource mobilization
observations.summary_quote: While the CCO had a comprehensive fundraising strategy and some activity tracking tools, there was a need for a comprehensive, accessible system that included information on planned initiatives, results of completed initiatives, and continuous evaluation of fundraising activities

observations.title: Partnership management
observations.summary_quote: The CCO lacked an overarching partnership strategy that was aligned with its internal capacity to fulfil assurance, monitoring, and partner capacity building requirements.

observations.title: Programme monitoring
observations.summary_quote: The lack of an office-wide monitoring plan, coupled with inadequate coordination and oversight of monitoring activities, resulted in insufficient programmatic monitoring coverage during the audited period.

observations.title: Cash transfers to implementing partners
observations.summary_quote: The limited availability of partners to conduct work for specific outcomes and in certain locations, combined with outdated partner risk profiles, led to the use of the DCT modality to transfer cash to high-risk partners. Several DCTs were outstanding for over six months

observations.title: HACT assurance
observations.summary_quote: Since the previous OIAI audit, the CCO had taken steps to strengthen HACT assurance, with a focus on internal capacity and oversight. While many improvements were noted, full implementation of all necessary measures was impeded by resource constraints.

observations.title: Supply planning, distribution and warehousing
observations.summary_quote: The CCO had not conducted logistics capacity assessments for key implementing partners that had received significant volumes of supplies, resulting in a lack of assurance of the partners’ capacity to receive, store and distribute UNICEF supplies.

observations.title: Construction
observations.summary_quote: The CCO did not obtain performance guarantees for construction works and did not exercise sufficient oversight of construction works conducted through CSOs.

observations.title: Service contracts
observations.summary_quote: Contracts were not always promptly closed upon expiry due to delays in pre-closure actions, such as contractor performance evaluations. Several contracts were issued after the contract start date and commencement of services, creating contractual and reputational risks for UNICEF.

agreed_actions.quote: The CCO should liaise with the regional office and/or UNICEF headquarters to explore and implement a cost-effective resource mobilization tracking system, either by acquiring sufficient licences for UNISON or developing an alternative solution.
Establish a structured partnership capacity strategy aligned with programmatic needs and the CCO’s internal capacity for assurance, monitoring, and partner capacity building.
Develop and implement a programme monitoring strategy, in consultation with different sections, to ensure that monitoring activities are complete and well-coordinated.

Implement the planned actions to update/correct partner risk profiles, plan partner capacity building as required, and assess the possibility of establishing a mechanism to minimize the use of the DCT modality for significant and high-risk partners.

Implement sustainable improvements in HACT assurance oversight through the HACT Committee, as planned, to ensure timely, risk-based HACT assurance activities and follow-up, including identification and recovery of ineligible expenditure.
Conduct logistics capacity assessments for implementing partners that receive significant or sensitive programme supplies.

Engage with the UNICEF local banking partner in the country to establish a simplified application process for performance guarantee with the aim of easing the financial burden on selected construction contractors.
Ensure that all actions required prior to closure of service contracts (including vendor performance evaluations) are conducted on a timely basis to allow prompt contract closure.

noteworthy_practices.quote: By developing resource mobilization strategies for both institutional and private sector funding, the CCO increased mobilization of funds through the retention of existing donors and the addition of new donors to the donor base.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

noteworthy_practices.quote: In January 2025, the eTools field monitoring module was adopted by the CCO and is expected to further improve the reporting and quality assurance of programme monitoring
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

title: 2025 OIAI Audit Report of Gabon Country Office

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management, or control processes were Partially Satisfactory, Improvement Needed, meaning they were generally adequate and functioning, but needed some improvement.

observations.title: Fraud risk management
observations.summary_quote: While the Country Office did conduct the 2025 annual fraud risk assessment, the audit team identified inconsistencies and duplications in the fraud risks listed in the Country Office Risk Register.

observations.title: Programme management
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office’s staffing and financial resources were not sufficiently aligned with the scale of planned activities, indicators, and expected results. The audit team also identified weaknesses in programme monitoring practices.

observations.title: Cash transfer and financial assurance
observations.summary_quote: The audit identified several control weaknesses in the management of cash transfers to implementing partners. Key control gaps included the use of alternate payees without proper oversight or due diligence, direct payments to implementing partners without sufficient supporting documentation (e.g., proof of service delivery)

observations.title: Procurement of institutional service contracts
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office did not have an adequate process to regularly review and revise the approved Supply Plan to reflect changes in stock levels and needs.

observations.title: Resource mobilization
observations.summary_quote: Two of the three Country Office programme outcomes are significantly underfunded, with no secured funding in the pipeline.

agreed_actions.quote: provide comprehensive training on fraud risk assessments and mitigation to all Country Office staff, with a particular focus on the designated fraud focal point
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Agreed Actions & Audit Ratings

agreed_actions.quote: align the Country Programme structure and priorities with available staffing and financial resources
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Agreed Actions & Audit Ratings

agreed_actions.quote: trengthen internal controls over cash transfers to implementing partners by ensuring that all direct payments are pre-approved by the Country Office and that cash liquidations are adequately supported with appropriate documentation
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Agreed Actions & Audit Ratings

agreed_actions.quote: evelop a robust process that requires the Supply Plan to be regularly reviewed and revised to reflect changes in stock levels and needs
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Agreed Actions & Audit Ratings

agreed_actions.quote: strengthen resource mobilization initiatives by developing private sector fundraising capacity and increasing advocacy and policy influence
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Agreed Actions & Audit Ratings

noteworthy_practices.quote: The Country Office has been effectively leveraging the Common United Nations Services under the Business Operations Strategy (BOS).
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

noteworthy_practices.quote: The Country Office has implemented several measures to monitor staff travel costs. For example, it maintains a monthly travel plan that is aligned with the field monitoring plan.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

title: 2025 OIAI Audit Report of the Burkina Faso Country Office

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management, or control processes were partly satisfactory, major improvement needed, meaning that the assessed governance, risk management, and control processes needed major improvement.

observations.title: Risk management
observations.summary_quote: There are gaps in risk identification in the Country Office risk register, which creates a risk of unaddressed vulnerabilities, aggregated risk categories that hinder the pinpointing of specific mitigating controls, and loosely defined and untracked action points that hinder accountability and the effective implementation of mitigating measures

observations.title: Cash transfers & HACT assurance
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office had US$ 2.6 million open balances in financial findings, with approximately 85 per cent or US$ 2.2 million aged for over three years. The management's follow-up on financial findings needed improvement.

observations.title: Supply planning, inventory and warehouse management and supply end-user monitoring
observations.summary_quote: Supply End User Monitoring has not been conducted since 2023, and logistics assessments were not conducted for major partners receiving supplies; the storage of supplies in partners' warehouses and their receipt by beneficiaries are not tracked.

observations.title: PSEA
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office’s PSEA oversight needs to be improved. The Country Office operates with numerous reporting channels without a formal tracking system to allow for adequate monitoring of reports.

observations.title: Programme monitoring and reporting of results
observations.summary_quote: The quality of monitoring activities needs strengthening as it did not provide sufficient oversight of the country programme implementation.

observations.title: Partnership management
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office lacks the proper documentation to explain the delay in finalising Programme Documents (PDs) and Simplified/Humanitarian Programme Documents (SPDs). Further, due to the country context, the Country Office often used the direct partner selection modality. However, these selections lacked adequate documentation of the rationale as to why the direct selection modality was used.

observations.title: Accountability to affected populations
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office lacks a formal structure to work on accountability to affected populations (AAP).

observations.title: Resource mobilization
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office has a comprehensive resource mobilization strategy outlining the current donor landscape and new institutional donors to be targeted. While suggested strategies for activities are in place, there is no clearly identified pathway as to how the Country Office will work to convert efforts into mobilized funds.

observations.title: Service Contracts
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office has implemented a comprehensive monitoring framework with adequate staff for direct construction projects, ensuring guarantees are in place and that progress payments are linked to certified completion milestones.

observations.title: Local Procurements
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office lacks sufficient internal control and oversight over the procurement of supplies. This includes deficiencies in the technical evaluation process, improper use of Long-Term Agreements (LTAs), and the absence of a price list for regularly procured items, which increases the risk of fraud and inefficiencies.

agreed_actions.quote: Conduct a comprehensive risk assessment to: a. Identify critical programmatic and operational risks relevant to the Country Office in the risk register; b. Break down aggregated risk categories to identify specific risks;
Review financial findings and collaborate with implementing partners to find adequate documentation to support expenses where relevant.
Conduct end-user monitoring for distributed supplies; ii. Assess logistic capacity for major implementing partners; iii. Promptly record any impaired stock in the corporate system
Improve and systematise the tracking of action plans at the Country Office level by assigning responsible staff to capture PSEA impact better
Develop a checklist for staff conducting visits and reviewers to evaluate before accepting report drafts
Conduct enhanced implementing partner due diligence, considering relevant risks identified in the Country Office risk registry
Create a formal structure to manage AAP and appoint a dedicated focal point; ii. Finalise the AAP strategy for 2025-2027.
Revise the current Resource Mobilization Strategy to clearly define resource mobilization initiatives, oversight and tracking mechanisms
Define precise and clear procurement requirements in terms of references, enabling service providers to better respond to Country Office requirements and facilitate more efficient competition

Implement strong oversight mechanisms to monitor that all end user monitoring, logistics assessment and recording of supplies are conducted efficiently and adequately; ii. Launch a local market assessment and establish LTAs for recurrent procurements.

noteworthy_practices.quote: During audit field work, the audit team met with various Burkinabe government partners, including the Ministry of Health and the Ministries of Primary and Secondary Education. They all commended the strong positive relationship with UNICEF as a key partner in developing capacity in respective sectors.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

title: 2025 OIAI Audit Report of the Eswatini Country Office

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management, or control processes were Partially Satisfactory, Improvement Needed meaning that the assessed governance, risk management, and control processes were generally adequate and functioning but needed improvement.

observations.title: Governance and accountability
observations.summary_quote: In addition to the statutory governance committees, the ECO had created several committees to address specific issues, resulting in a high number of committees for a relatively small country office, some with overlapping mandates.

observations.title: Risk management including fraud risk management
observations.summary_quote: The ECO’s risk assessment process did not adequately ensure ongoing monitoring and review of all key risks, such as risks related to office affordability, assurance on the use of UNICEF funds, child safeguarding, and potential fraud and waste of programme supplies. Furthermore, the ECO did not consistently report suspected fraud cases to UNICEF OIAI in a timely manner, as required.

observations.title: Resource mobilization
observations.summary_quote: The ECO had not developed a plan to mitigate funding gaps for a key outcome of the country programme.

observations.title: Protection from Sexual Exploitation and Abuse
observations.summary_quote: The ECO had not conducted a robust PSEA risk assessment and most of the activities identified in the PSEA workplan for 2025 were unfunded. The quality of PSEA risk assessments conducted for implementing partners was below expectations, as final ratings for partners were not always consistent with the detailed supporting comments.

observations.title: Management of implementing partners
observations.summary_quote: The ECO tended to directly select known CSO partners instead of using the default competitive ‘open selection’ modality, without a sound rationale.

observations.title: Cash transfers to implementing partners
observations.summary_quote: The ECO incorrectly classified partner-related expenditure amounting to US$737,118 with the result that financial reports produced from corporate systems were incorrect.

observations.title: HACT financial assurance
observations.summary_quote: The ECO’s approach to HACT assurance planning was not sufficiently risk-based to ensure that assurance activities were proportionate to the overall level of financial exposure.

observations.title: Management and oversight of supplies
observations.summary_quote: The ECO facilitated a significant donation of supplies from UNICEF Supply Division for a government implementing partner, without validating the needs of the country or assessing the partner’s capacity to manage and distribute the supplies.

agreed_actions.quote: Evaluate whether non-statutory committees are still needed and review the terms of reference for the non-statutory committees which are retained.

Establish regular, comprehensive reviews of risks and mitigating actions to ensure proactive management of significant and emerging risks and include risk discussions as a standing agenda item in CMT meetings.
Enhance resource mobilization capacity, including through targeted training for the current focal point and leveraging support from ESARO.
Ensure that robust PSEA risk assessments are conducted and that all risks are identified, assessed and appropriate mitigating measures taken.

Strengthen the partnership approval process by ensuring that open selection is the default modality, unless justification for direct selection consistent with the established UNICEF standards is documented
Conduct refresher training for staff and partners on expense coding, as per the latest DAPM guidance, and strengthen post-transaction review controls.
Conduct and finalize the delayed (2023) partner audit and incorporate any lessons learned from this to strengthen HACT assurance planning and monitoring activities.
Report the loss of UNICEF-donated supplies to the regional office, Supply Division and other relevant headquarter offices.

noteworthy_practices.quote: The audit noted a high level of partner contribution to projects that are implemented by CSO partners. title: 2025 OIAI Audit Report on the Congo Country Office

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management, or control processes were Partially Satisfactory, Improvement Needed, meaning they were generally adequate and functioning, but needed some improvement.

observations.title: Construction projects
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office did not make an update of needs identified in the proposal before implementing construction projects, leading to the construction of boreholes in some schools that already had functional ones.

observations.title: Risk management
observations.summary_quote: While the Country Office established a risk management committee and conducted a fraud-inclusive risk assessment in 2025, the audit team noted gaps in identifying, assessing, mitigating and monitoring some programme-related risks.

observations.title: Resource mobilization
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office lacks adequate financial resources to sustain some of the programme outputs approved in the 2020–2026 Country Programme Document (CPD).

observations.title: Cash transfers to implementing partners
observations.summary_quote: Despite high-risk ratings of implementing partners, outstanding cash transfers and ineligible expenses, the Country Office continued to rely on direct cash transfers (DCTs), increasing the risk of delayed liquidation and potential fund diversion.

observations.title: Financial assurance activities
observations.summary_quote: As of September 2025, ineligible expenses totalling US$569,412 million remained outstanding, with 71 per cent (US$403,146) overdue for more than two years. Additionally, not all internal control findings, including high-priority issues, were entered into eTools or adequately tracked.

observations.title: Programme management
observations.summary_quote: the auditors found that approximately 75 per cent of the funds transferred to government implementing partners during the period of audit were used for allowances and operational expenses, costs that could be the responsibility of the government for more efficiency.

observations.title: Procurement of services
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office’s vendor selection and contract award processes lacked transparency, were insufficiently documented and failed to provide value for money for UNICEF. Combined with insufficient oversight of car rentals, these weaknesses significantly increased the risk of fraud.

observations.title: Warehouse management and distribution of supplies
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office did not consistently update its inventory records or obtain acknowledgements on supplies transferred to implementing partners, which increases the risk of fraud and misappropriation of supplies.

agreed_actions.quote: Strengthen construction planning and oversight by conducting a thorough site assessment before initiating construction projects, issuing contracts in a timely manner, and ensuring that all construction projects are completed within the designated grant validity periods.

strengthen resource mobilization initiatives by leveraging the private sector and increasing advocacy and policy influence within the public and private sectors, as well as the government.
Strengthen oversight of cash transfers to implementing partners by contacting the Division of Finance and Administration Management to revise the office release strategy
Strengthen oversight of financial assurance activities by ensuring that all recommendations are systematically reviewed, documented in eTools and followed up in a timely manner

Redefine its priorities as part of the preparation of the new Country Programme Document and ensure that financial resources are directed towards sustainable capacity building and institutional reforms.

Strengthen oversight of procurement and contract management by ensuring that vendor selection and contract award processes are transparent, provide value for money to UNICEF and are fully and properly documented.

strengthen its warehouse and inventory management processes by ensuring that all inventory movements are promptly recorded in the inventory system and that supplies transferred to implementing partners are systematically acknowledged upon receipt.

title: 2025 OIAI Audit Report on the Haiti Country Office

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management, or control processes were partially satisfactory, major improvement needed, meaning that the assessed governance, risk management, or control processes needed major improvement.

observations.title: Risk Management

observations.title: Internal Control Self-Assessment
observations.summary_quote: The HCO’s internal control self-assessment indicated that all 39 controls assessed were adequately managed; however, upon further review, the audit identified several inaccurate assessments of internal control effectiveness, which could limit the HCO’s ability to mitigate exposure and subject it to additional risk.

observations.title: Resource Mobilization
observations.summary_quote: Despite fundraising efforts, the HCO lacked an updated resource mobilization strategy (RM strategy), thereby impacting its ability to secure and manage funding.

observations.title: Oversight and Monitoring of Training
observations.summary_quote: Despite the HCO's efforts to deliver well-adapted training materials and sessions, significant gaps were identified in oversight and monitoring of training. Some staff have not completed the mandatory prevention of sexual exploitation and abuse (PSEA), anti-fraud, procurement, and security training in a timely manner, despite a complex operating environment.

observations.title: Cash Transfers
observations.summary_quote: The HCO relies heavily on direct cash transfers (DCTs) to high-risk partners, despite their limited financial capacity, increasing the risk of misuse or fraud.

observations.title: Monitoring
observations.summary_quote: The HCO’s monitoring activities showed significant gaps, including low completion rates and limited use of mandatory eTools monitoring systems, which reduced its ability to identify and address issues and maintain transparency.

observations.title: Indirect Construction
observations.summary_quote: The HCO did not exercise sufficient oversight of construction projects carried out by CSOs. Construction capacity assessments were not completed.

observations.title: Emergency Response
observations.summary_quote: The preparedness plan was completed outside the EPP due to the changing environment. There was low adoption of emergency procedures in recruitment and partnerships due to

observations.title: Security
observations.locator: Observation 9 (redacted per Executive Board decision EB2012/12)

observations.title: Human Resources Management
observations.summary_quote: The HCO experienced significant human resources challenges, including staffing cuts from 172 to 105, prolonged vacancies, and a budget reduction that impacted core functions.

observations.title: Viability of Delivering the Country Programme
observations.summary_quote: Given the past, present, and ongoing security and related challenges facing the HCO, the most recent budget and staffing reductions, and the changes resulting from UNICEF’s FFI, there is a significant risk that the HCO is unviable and will be unable to meet its CPD 2023-2027 goals.

agreed_actions.quote: The HCO agrees to strengthen oversight of the risk management process to ensure the review of the quality and completeness of risk mitigation measures.
The HCO agrees to improve the internal control self-assessment process to ensure that control gaps are identified and targeted mitigation actions are developed and implemented.
Finalize the RM strategy and related action plan and ensure they clearly define the roles, responsibilities and accountabilities for each position and that it is aligned with the advocacy strategy.

Strengthen oversight mechanisms to systematically track, document, and verify compliance with mandatory training requirements for both internal staff and external stakeholders (e.g., partners, service providers).
Use a risk-based approach when selecting the modality to transfer cash to high-risk partners, considering the effectiveness of risk-mitigating actions, such as monitoring and assurance activities.
Develop office-wide monitoring plans to ensure minimum risk-based programmatic visit monitoring and financial spot checks are completed.
Strengthen oversight over indirect construction projects. ii. Provide staff training to improve their understanding of the UNICEF Procedure for Construction Projects.
The HCO agrees to implement the recommendations included in the evaluation of UNICEF Level 3 emergency in Haiti report.
Maintain accurate records on the recruitment of staff.
The HCO, in collaboration with the RO and relevant HQ divisions, agrees to review the viability of its operational model to achieve its planned programme goals.

title: 2026 OIAI Audit Report on the Chad Country Office

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management, or control processes were Partially Satisfactory, Major Improvement Needed

observations.title: Oversight and accountability
observations.summary_quote: The Chad Country Office has faced vacancies in critical leadership positions, which were addressed through interim appointments of officers in charge. These gaps could have an impact on programme delivery.

observations.title: Fraud risk and risk management
observations.summary_quote: Governance, risk and compliance processes in the Chad Country Office require significant strengthening to address vulnerabilities in fraud prevention, risk management and internal controls.

observations.title: Decentralization strategy
observations.summary_quote: Challenges in the decentralization process, including partial adherence to the accountability framework, variability in monitoring practices and the absence of standardized tools, may affect the Country Office’s capacity to consistently deliver services at the decentralized level.

observations.title: Cross-border humanitarian responses
observations.summary_quote: The absence of formal agreements, joint financial and logistic oversight, and risk escalation protocols may result in unclear roles, fragmented cost-sharing and untracked risks that can affect timely response and increase exposure to supply chain disruptions.

observations.title: Strategic funding and structural alignment
observations.summary_quote: Uncertainties in funding and data inconsistencies may weaken the reliability of staffing plans for the 2026 programme cycle. While efforts were made to align staffing with projected resources, reliance on high-probability income and a few major donors increases financial risk.

observations.title: Work planning and result formulation
observations.summary_quote: Improving the formulation of programme indicators and the integration of Accountability to Affected Populations across both humanitarian and development programming would strengthen strategic programme management, enhance community engagement and align more closely with UNICEF’s results-based management and AAP principles.

observations.title: Implementing partnership management
observations.summary_quote: Lack of standardized scoring, missing capacity assessments, inadequate monitoring of CSO-implemented projects and gaps in PSEA oversight have increased the office’s exposure to financial, operational and reputational risks, undermining transparency and accountability.

observations.title: Direct cash transfers to implementing partners
observations.summary_quote: There are challenges in ensuring timely and accountable implementation of the Country Office’s cash transfer programme due to weaknesses in financial oversight and follow-up.

observations.title: Construction contract management
observations.summary_quote: Weaknesses in construction contract management and oversight have significantly impacted the timely and accountable delivery of infrastructure projects. Delays in execution, absence of performance guarantees, and incomplete documentation have heightened financial and operational risks.

observations.title: Supply chain management
observations.summary_quote: Enhancing supply chain processes – by reducing delivery delays, improving storage conditions and strengthening quality assurance – will help the office minimize avoidable costs, safeguard supply quality and ensure resources deliver maximum value for programme results.

observations.title: Cash-based assistance
observations.summary_quote: Strengthening the monitoring and oversight of the cash-based assistance programme could improve the reliability of payments and the protection of beneficiary data.

observations.title: Service contracts management
observations.summary_quote: Procurement of service performance was impacted by incomplete and outdated supply planning, which limited traceability of contracts and led to delays, inefficiencies and compliance challenges.

observations.title: Grants management
observations.summary_quote: Grant implementation performance was impacted by late contracting and cash transfers disbursed near grant expiry, without sufficient assurance of timely execution or donor approval. These practices increased the risk of delays, ineligible expenditures and reputational exposure.

observations.title: Programme monitoring and financial assurance
observations.summary_quote: Delays in reporting, weak monitoring practices, and limited visibility over financial and supply-related activities have undermined the effectiveness of programme monitoring and assurance in the Chad Country Office.

agreed_actions.quote: capture lessons learned from past situations and stagger leadership transitions, minimizing simultaneous vacancies and maintaining continuity
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Agreed Actions & Audit Ratings

agreed_actions.quote: Update and fully implement the fraud strategy, incorporating regional recommendations and integrating progress reviews into management processes.
Review the implementation of the finalized accountability and delegation frameworks and ensure alignment with field-level operations through integrated planning and supervision tools.

strengthen assurance and oversight activities by updating terms of reference and ensure there are adequate reviews of partner budgeting practices and verification of fund utilization at the field level through targeted monitoring and follow-up.
Implement alternative mitigation measures with support from DFAM to substitute for performance guarantees when acceptable banking partners are not available.
Implement a delivery schedule with contingency plans to prevent delays and ensure timely distribution of supplies.
Verify beneficiary eligibility during field monitoring. ii. Ensure direct access to and periodic testing of grievance mechanisms.
Implement measures to ensure timely budget allocation and regular updates to the supply plan to enhance traceability of contracting activities.
Revise the country programme resource mobilization strategy in response to the rapidly evolving donor landscape
Conduct micro-assessments for all high-value partners before fund disbursement and integrate results into a risk-informed assurance strategy.

title: 2026 Internal Audit of Travel Management

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management, or control processes were partially satisfactory, major improvement needed, meaning that the weaknesses or deficiencies identified could have a materially negative impact on the performance of the audited entity, area, activity or process.

observations.title: Governance structure
observations.summary_quote: UNICEF’s governance structure for managing travel included duplicate and overlapping efforts and gaps, particularly regarding accountabilities for monitoring and oversight of travel.

observations.title: Policy framework
observations.summary_quote: There are duplications, gaps and inconsistencies in the 13 key documents that UNICEF uses to manage travel, underscoring the need to assess them to ensure the procedures are relevant and current, and that they support UNICEF’s business and strategic objectives.

observations.title: Risk management
observations.summary_quote: UNICEF does not specifically assess the risk of fraud associated with travel. Where travel-related control weaknesses are identified through risk assessment, they are not effectively used to manage risk across the organization.

observations.title: Information for decision-making
observations.summary_quote: The advanced trip balance indicated in UNICEF’s travel dashboard did not reconcile with the comparative balance in VISION. The discrepancy resulted from travel agent payments not being included in the calculation.

observations.title: Oversight and monitoring
observations.summary_quote: UNICEF has insufficient assurance that the funds being spent on travel are correct and for the intended purposes. Most travel transactions in UNICEF are not subject to any secondary review or monitoring.

observations.title: Control weaknesses
observations.summary_quote: Several travel control weaknesses were noted, including inadequate travel planning, unclear budget owner responsibilities in the travel procedures and guidance notes, and weak controls for approving head of office travel.

observations.title: Vendor management and value for money
observations.summary_quote: Although Headquarters independently assessed value for money from its travel management company and regularly monitored its performance, this was not the case for the more than 300 travel agents used by regional and country offices. This was particularly concerning given that travel from these offices accounted for 91 per cent of all UNICEF travel expenditures.

agreed_actions.quote: The Division of Financial and Administrative Management should, in consultation with all relevant stakeholders, strengthen the governance structure by identifying an enterprise-wide process owner for travel management and clarifying the roles and accountabilities for all relevant stakeholders.
Agree on a timeline to identify all obsolete policies, procedures and guidance related to the management of travel and remove them from the regulatory framework
should raise awareness and assessments of travel-related risks, including fraud, at the enterprise level.
The Division of Financial and Administrative Management should collaborate with the Digital Impact Division
clarify the points of checks and balances, and procedures to support oversight accountability in the travel procedures; ensure due diligence is conducted on all one-time vendors

clarify the budget owner’s responsibilities in the relevant travel procedures; ensure heads of offices’ travel requests are approved by the relevant approving authority or their delegate to ensure transparency and objectivity

ensure all daily subsistence allowance calculations are performed in the travel module and that any amendments to the travel are made to the original travel authorization to facilitate monitoring and review

noteworthy_practices.quote: The Division of Financial and Administrative Management (DFAM) launched the e-Travel application in July 2024 to streamline the travel administration process.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

noteworthy_practices.quote: DFAM rolled out the lump sum airfare (AIRINC) database in December 2024 to provide lump sum airfare quotes to improve transparency and speed up the administrative process for travel.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

noteworthy_practices.quote: In late 2024, the comptroller changed the purchase of all New York Headquarters tickets to refundable, reducing UNICEF’s spending on costly change and cancellation fees.

title: 2026 OIAI Audit Report on the Rwanda Country Office

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management, or control processes were Partially Satisfactory, Improvement Needed, meaning they were generally adequate and functioning, but needed some improvement.

observations.title: Risk management
observations.summary_quote: About 33 per cent of non-staff did not complete mandatory fraud, ethics and integrity training courses as required, and not all IPs received mandatory fraud training to raise their awareness and understanding.

observations.title: Management of implementing partners
observations.summary_quote: The RCO lacked oversight of VAT refunds collected by IPs. Additionally, the RCO did not follow up to ensure IPs comply with VAT requirements, creating a risk that funds could be misused.

observations.title: Programme supplies
observations.summary_quote: Government IPs did not specify the quantities of supplies required in their supply request letters to the RCO, increasing the risk of waste, and misuse of resources. There were inconsistencies between the consignees listed on purchase orders and the entities that ultimately received the supplies

observations.title: Financial assurance activities
observations.summary_quote: The audit identified weaknesses in the RCO’s spot check processes, including the failure to flag ineligible expenditure by external auditors, a lack of follow-up on financial findings, and the closure of action points without appropriate supporting documentation, which increased the risk of fund misuse, non-recovery of financial findings, and potential fraud.

observations.title: Service contracts
observations.summary_quote: Two contracts, over the US$100,000 threshold, were not reviewed by the CRC. One was the lease agreement, which was renewed without an evaluation of the performance of the vendor.

observations.title: Prevention of sexual exploitation and abuse
observations.summary_quote: There were limited resources in the RCO to ensure the implementation of PSEA goals. In addition, there was inconsistent monitoring of the implementation of the action plans of IPs with medium capacity.

agreed_actions.quote: Strengthen the monitoring and oversight mechanisms to systematically track, document, and verify compliance with mandatory training requirements for non-staff (e.g., United Nations Volunteers and consultants).

Strengthen oversight of VAT refunds by continuing to train CSO partners on VAT refund requirements, incorporating VAT provisions into PDs, and establishing a follow‑up mechanism to ensure that VAT refunds collected are remitted back in a timely manner.

Strengthen oversight of supplies transferred to IPs by ensuring accurate consignee information and requiring government IPs to use standardized supply request forms that clearly specify item descriptions, quantities, and intended use.
Strengthen oversight of financial assurance activities by ensuring that all recommendations are systematically reviewed, documented in eTools, and followed up in a timely manner
agreed_actions.locator: Executive Summary — Agreed Actions & Audit Ratings

agreed_actions.quote: Strengthen the understanding of the supply procedures of its staff to ensure that all contracts, meeting the threshold, are reviewed by the CRC.
Provide adequate resources to ensure the implementation of PSEA programmatic goals.

noteworthy_practices.quote: The RCO had a communication and advocacy strategy and a resource mobilization and leveraging strategy covering the CPD period. Together, they form a cohesive plan for the RCO to enhance child well-being through strategic communication, advocacy, and resource mobilization. The RCO budget for 2025 and 2026 was fully met.

title: 2026 OIAI Audit Report on the Senegal Country Office

overall_conclusion.quote: management or control processes were Partially Satisfactory, Improvement Needed, meaning that the weaknesses or deficiencies identified are unlikely to have a materially negative impact on the performance of the audited entity, area, activity or process.

observations.title: Governance and operational structure
observations.summary_quote: The Senegal Country Office lacks a documented governance and accountability framework for major operational changes, including Common Services, the Independent Project Unit, the UN House Management Unit and the transition to the Common Back Office.

observations.title: Programme sustainability and financial resilience
observations.summary_quote: The Country Programme faces significant sustainability and financial risks due to reliance on UNICEF internal resources and the lack of exit and financing frameworks.

observations.title: Risk management
observations.summary_quote: There are gaps in the Country Office’s risk management framework, including an incomplete risk register, missing mitigation measures and weak monitoring systems. Critical risks remain unaddressed, causing delays and inefficiencies.

observations.title: Resource mobilization and grant management
observations.summary_quote: The Senegal Country Office’s Resource Mobilization Strategy (2024–2028) has not been updated to reflect major funding shifts. This lack of update, combined with low proposal success rates and donor retention challenges, increases the risks of funding shortfalls

observations.title: Management of construction activities
observations.summary_quote: Delays in documentation and quality issues in the management of construction-related activities – including planning, procurement, oversight and partner coordination – have affected timely delivery of programme results and increased operational and financial risks for the Country Office.

observations.title: Cash transfers and financial assurance activities
observations.summary_quote: Weaknesses in cash transfer management and financial assurance – including delays, inaccurate reviews, inconsistent modality application and insufficient follow‑up on ineligible expenses –undermine compliance with HACT procedures and UNICEF Financial Rules and Regulations, increase fiduciary risk and delay programme implementation.

observations.title: Programme monitoring
observations.summary_quote: There are opportunities to strengthen programme monitoring processes to improve data reliability, accountability and overall compliance, thereby enabling the office to deliver more effective, accountable programming.

agreed_actions.quote: Develop an addendum/annex to the CPMP defining roles for Common Services, the UNHMU and the CBO.

Develop and formalize a sustainability and exit framework that consolidates existing programme‑level sustainability practices into an office‑wide approach with phased transition considerations and government financing pathways

Strengthen the functioning of the Risk Committee by clarifying roles/ownership and integrating the UN House/CBO risks into a shared, regularly reviewed register (with Country Management Team oversight).
Revise the Resource Mobilization Strategy during the Mid‑Term Review to reflect funding priorities and gaps.
Strengthen governance and technical oversight for construction projects, including clear roles, documentation requirements and mandatory technical supervision.
Enhance quality assurance and timeliness by implementing standardized review checklists and monitoring FACE processing against service standards.
Enhance supervisory review of programme visits, spot checks and financial assurance activities and implement an AAP strategy.

noteworthy_practices.quote: This sector-wide mechanism was created to monitor and coordinate all budgetary support in education. It was established under UNICEF’s leadership with the aims of reducing fragmented financing and strengthening governance and accountability.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions (Comité de Suivi des Appuis Budgétaires)

noteworthy_practices.quote: Institutionalized councils at the local level allow children to participate in governance and decision-making.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions (Conseils Municipaux des Enfants)

noteworthy_practices.quote: Country Office was the first globally to submit a proposal to the Green Climate Fund, supporting the positioning of the UNICEF Senegal Country Office on climate finance.

title: 2026 OIAI Audit Report on the Sri Lanka Country Office

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management, or control processes were Partially Satisfactory, Improvement Needed meaning that the assessed governance, risk management, and control processes were generally adequate and functioning but needed improvement.

observations.title: Risk management and fraud risk assessments
observations.summary_quote: The audit identified gaps in the country office’s risk management framework, particularly in fraud risk assessment. Fraud risks were rated “very low” despite Sri Lanka’s high Corruption Perceptions Index. Vendor due diligence was weak, with most vendors not registered in UNGM

observations.title: Protection from Sexual Exploitation and Abuse
observations.summary_quote: There were delays in the implementation of some planned activities which affected the full rollout of UNICEF’s safeguarding standards. There was limited community awareness and safe reporting mechanisms, increasing the risk of SEA allegations going unreported.

observations.title: Programme and field monitoring
observations.summary_quote: A cohesive monitoring plan did not exist beyond HACT minimum required programmatic visits that includes other risk informed determinants such as PSEA and AAP. Audit sample testing identified recurring quality gaps in programmatic assurance documentation.

observations.title: Implementing partnerships management
observations.summary_quote: The audit noted that CSO partnerships were primarily established through direct selection, with only three PDs created following an open selection process. Over-reliance on direct selection may limit opportunities to diversify partnerships.

observations.title: Cash transfers to implementing partners
observations.summary_quote: The audit observed fragmentation of cash transfers, where certain partners submitted numerous low-value FACE forms, creating operational inefficiencies and reducing assurance coverage during spot checks. Additionally, the audit identified misclassification of approximately US$1 million in HACT-related expenditure

observations.title: HACT financial assurance activities
observations.summary_quote: The audit noted that the country office generally complied with HACT requirements, completing all planned assurance activities for 2024 and most for 2025. However, the level of assurance derived from these activities was limited due to low testing coverage during spot checks

observations.title: Supply chain management
observations.summary_quote: The audit noted weaknesses across supply planning, distribution visibility, customs clearance, warehousing oversight, procurement controls, and CRC functioning.

agreed_actions.quote: Revise the methodology used in assessing the risk of fraud to go beyond the quantitative attributes by incorporating qualitative attributes such as the country's Corruption Perceptions Index, and red flags identified from other sources.
Expedite implementation of pending PSEA action plan activities, including community consultations, display of reporting materials, and communication to CSOs on UNICEF’s focal points.

Develop a consolidated, risk-based monitoring plan endorsed by the CMT that integrates all section monitoring activities, defines coverage targets by risk/value, and includes determinants such as quality, sustainability, PSEA and AAP.

Adopt an open selection modality wherever feasible to ensure that partner selection is conducted in a manner that demonstrates fairness, transparency, and value for money, even if the same partner is ultimately retained.
Promote integrated financial management among the affected implementing partners by advocating for consolidated cash transfer requests/reporting across units within the same entity.
Establish a risk-based approach towards the assurance process considering key risk factors like the volume of expenditure reported by an implementing partner.

Enhance the supply planning process by ensuring regular updates, systematic monitoring of implementation, and early involvement of the supply section in programme and partnership planning discussions involving procurement.

noteworthy_practices.quote: ADEPT is a voluntary programme offered by UNICEF Supply Division in partnership with the Global Trade Alliance, to countries experiencing customs clearance inefficiencies or delays. Sri Lanka volunteered for inclusion in this programme.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

noteworthy_practices.quote: The country office’s building project has an achievement of 40 per cent predicted energy savings.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions

title: 2026 OIAI Audit Report on the Chile Country Office

overall_conclusion.quote: OIAI concluded that the assessed governance, risk management or control processes were Partially Satisfactory, Improvement Needed, meaning that the assessed governance, risk management and control processes were generally adequate and functioning but needed improvement.

observations.title: Governance
observations.summary_quote: During the period under review, the Country Office operated with generally sound governance arrangements while transitioning towards a self-financing office model. Although oversight structures and change management initiatives were in place, the transition introduced new governance challenges related to income concentration, evolving oversight expectations, and the integration of risk and assurance information

observations.title: Risk and fraud risk management
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office demonstrated generally sound risk and fraud risk management practices, supported by a strong speak-up culture, risk-based partner due diligence and timely incident response. These arrangements helped mitigate operational and reputational risks. However, reliance on a limited partner base in higher-risk contexts and the presence of integrity red flags in certain vendor and partner relationships

observations.title: Private sector fundraising
observations.locator: Observation 3 (redacted per Executive Board decision EB2012/12)

observations.title: Harmonized approach to cash transfers
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office exceeded its coverage targets in applying the HACT assurance arrangements for financial and programmatic monitoring of implementing partners. However, delays in addressing high-risk micro-assessment recommendations and weaknesses in the quality and documentation of some programmatic visits indicate gaps in the effectiveness of HACT implementation

observations.title: Protection from sexual exploitation and abuse
observations.summary_quote: The Country Office has established solid PSEA awareness and coordination arrangements; however, reliance on a focal point with multiple additional responsibilities, and increased engagement with service providers and community-facing actors, create a risk of uneven coverage.

agreed_actions.quote: Conduct periodic scenario and stress-testing exercises addressing potential disruptions to domestic fundraising income, reputational events and adverse regulatory changes, and use the results to strengthen contingency planning and decision-making related to the self-financing model.
Strengthen fraud risk management of higher-risk partners by reinforcing fraud prevention, reporting and monitoring arrangements to align with their risk profile.
Ensure timely sharing of micro-assessment and spot-check reports with implementing partners.
Assess the adequacy of the time allocated to the PSEA focal point to discharge their duties.

noteworthy_practices.quote: The Country Office developed and implemented a detailed transition planning framework covering governance, staffing, communications and operational continuity to support its move towards a self-financing model.
noteworthy_practices.locator: Executive Summary — Summary of Observations and Agreed Actions
